 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 16 • Private sector appetite: Private financial institutions are increasingly open to nuclear, especially SMRs, provided there is strong government support, predictable cash flows, and robust risk-sharing mechanisms. The emergence of green and transition bonds for nuclear (e.g., EDF, Bruce Power, Constellation) is a positive signal. However, reputational risk and policy uncertainty remain concerns for some investors. Renewable electrons are less costly than nuclear power when compared. Renewables coupled with storage capacity competes with nuclear capacity • Competition with alternatives: While renewables are cheaper on a pure LCOE basis, their intermittency and the costs of grid integration, storage, and backup capacity mean that nuclear remains essential for system reliability and decarbonisation. Gas with CCS, while a competitor, faces its own cost, technology and policy risks. In many European markets, nuclear is now seen as complementary to renewables rather than a competitor. Key hurdles for developing new nuclear power and solutions Construction risks and cost overruns have been the norm for nuclear projects and increasingly so over recent decades • Construction risk and cost overruns: Lengthy construction times, cost overruns, and regulatory complexity have hindered projects, not only in Western Europe but across the world, especially when unproven features are introduced in reactor designs and construction risks increase accordingly, since there is less or no operating experience. The main drivers of these risks are the complexity and scale inherent in such projects. Unlike other energy-generation methods, nuclear initiatives must meet exceptionally rigorous regulatory oversight, safety protocols, and technical standards. In addition, the often-debated nature of nuclear power can create further political and regulatory challenges. Solutions include design standardisation, serial construction, modularisation (SMRs) and improved project management. “Five years” is seldom five years when building a nuclear plant A review of nuclear projects classified as “under construction” as of 1 July 2025 reveals a sector marked by persistent uncertainty and structural delays, despite the continued use of five-year construction timelines in many project proposals. On average, the 63 reactors currently being built have already spent more than five years under construction, and many remain far from completion. While this average is slightly lower than a year earlier, it does not signal improved performance so much as reflect the addition of recently started projects that have not yet had time to run into difficulties.

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 17 Construction time of units started and connected to grid between 2015-24 (yrs) Country Units Mean time Minimum Maximum China 37 6.3 4.1 10 Russia 8 16.2 8.1 35.1 South Korea 5 8.7 6.4 10.5 Pakistan 4 5.6 5.5 5.8 UAE 4 8.3 8 8.6 India 3 12.5 10.1 14.2 US 3 21.5 10.1 42.8 Belarus 2 8 7 8.9 Finland 1 16.6 16.6 16.6 France 1 17.1 17.1 17.1 Slovakia 1 38.1 38.1 38.1 Total 69 9.4 4.1 42.8 Source: WNISR Repeat construction and lessons learned allow for cheaper and faster projects Delays are widespread and, in many cases, systematic. All reactors under construction in at least six of the eleven building countries have experienced significant schedule slippages, often measured in years rather than months. More than one-third of all ongoing projects are formally documented as delayed, and the status of many others remains uncertain. Most reactors still described as “on time” began construction only within the past three years, and the majority of these are in China. Given the limited transparency and inconsistent public reporting on Chinese projects, it is difficult to assess whether these units are genuinely progressing according to plan or have simply not yet reached stages where delays become visible. Supplier choice is also a geopolitical choice Geopolitical factors further complicate the outlook. Projects based on Russian technology or involving Russian contractors face growing risks due to sanctions and disrupted supply chains. In most countries where such projects are underway, delays are already documented, with sanctions explicitly cited in several cases. Over the past year, the situation has deteriorated further, with additional delays reported across multiple countries and only a small number of projects still lacking confirmed schedule slippages. The cheque is in the mail… soon… The gap between planned and actual delivery is also evident in recent reactor startups. Of the reactors expected to begin operation in 2024, only half were connected to the grid on schedule, while the remainder were pushed back by at least a year. Several emblematic projects highlight the extreme duration nuclear construction can reach: some units in Slovakia and Iran trace their origins back 4-5 decades, having undergone long suspensions before resuming work. Others in India have remained officially “under construction” for more than ten years, underscoring how project status labels often obscure the true length and complexity of nuclear development. Learning curves important along with consistency in policies Looking beyond individual cases, historical data confirm that long construction times are no longer anomalies but the norm. While reactors completed in the 1970s and 1980s showed relatively consistent build times, the past two decades have been characterised by wide variation and frequent overruns. Reactors that started up between 2022 and 2024 took, on average, nearly 11 years to complete, and none entered operation on schedule. Even China, which performs better than most countries, rarely achieves construction times below seven years. Over the past decade, global average construction durations have stabilised around ten years, reflecting not only technical challenges but also the cumulative impact of regulatory processes, financing negotiations, licensing, and broader political and legal hurdles. From an analytical perspective, the evidence suggests that short, predictable nuclear construction timelines remain the exception rather than the rule. Both supply chains for technologies but also for the creation of skilled labour to build and operate nuclear plants are essential to achieve new capacity • Supply chain and workforce constraints: Europe’s nuclear supply chain and skilled workforce have diminished over decades. The aging workforce and the decline in the number of nuclear professionals under average age present a risk for both the future construction and operation of nuclear power plants as well as the decommissioning of older nuclear plants. Scaling up requires coordinated investment in manufacturing, training, and research and development. The future of the global, but also the European, nuclear industry relies heavily on the strength and stability of its supply chains. A resilient supply chain can adapt quickly to disruptions by using flexible contingency plans and accurate forecasting across all its stages. To support this, governments should avoid “stop‑and‑go” policy cycles, which make it difficult for any industry, especially those requiring long‑term planning, to commit to major supply‑chain investments. Careful, early planning is essential, including finding the right mix of global and local suppliers for each part of the chain. France plans to hire 10,000 workers annually over the next decade, putting the spotlight on the need to 

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 18 provide training and education relevant to the needs of the sector. Long-term investments require long-term policy support • Regulatory and policy uncertainty: Divergent national policies and shifting regulations create uncertainty. Streamlined and harmonised frameworks like UK planning reforms and the EU SMR Industrial Alliance are helpful to enable and reduce costs for projects. Nuclear power is now part of the EU Taxonomy • Financing and bankability: Capital intensity and long payback periods challenge commercial financing. Government support via guarantees, CfDs, RAB models, and direct investment is critical. Green and transition bonds and sustainable finance inclusion expand capital pools. The screening criteria focus on two key areas: greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions and activities that must “Do No Significant Harm” (DNSH). For GHG emissions, nuclear energy projects are required to keep life-cycle emissions below 100 grams of CO₂ equivalent per kilowatt-hour. The DNSH criteria address safety, regulatory compliance, and the sound management of radioactive waste and decommissioning. This classification has helped support the use of green bonds, financial instruments in which the proceeds are dedicated to funding eligible green projects, by nuclear operators within the European Union. One prominent example is EDF, which has issued multiple green bonds to support activities linked to nuclear power. • Public acceptance: While public opinion is improving due to energy security and climate concerns, opposition persists. Public acceptance relates both to the operations of nuclear capacity and the waste management process and subsequent storage required for spent nuclear fuel. Transparent communication, safety culture, and community engagement are vital. Fuel supply and processing capacity for key elements of the supply chain • Fuel supply security: Europe’s dependence on Russian enrichment and fuel services is a strategic risk. Diversification efforts include investments in domestic enrichment (Orano, Urenco) and development of new fuel types like HALEU for SMRs. According to the Euratom Supply Agency, the European Union now sources nuclear materials and related products from a broad and diversified set of suppliers. In 2024, total imports amounted to around 14,000 tonnes. As in the previous year, roughly one‑third of these imports came from Canada, while Kazakhstan supplied about one‑quarter. Russia’s share declined further in 2024, whereas imports from Australia and China increased. Decommissioning and waste management plans must also be developed • Waste management and decommissioning: Progress on geological repositories is underway in Sweden, Finland, France, and Switzerland, but long-term waste management remains a political challenge requiring robust funding and governance. Under the Radioactive Waste and Spent Fuel Management Directive, each EU member state must establish a national framework for managing spent nuclear fuel and radioactive waste. These requirements stem from the Euratom Treaty. Countries are obligated to develop and implement national programmes that ensure the safe and responsible handling of these materials. In line with Euratom’s waste-management rules, the EU periodically publishes inventory data on radioactive waste and spent fuel. All EU member states generate radioactive waste, and 17 of them also manage spent fuel within their territories. Because of the radiological characteristics and potential hazards associated with these materials, their safe management is essential at every stage of the nuclear lifecycle. This material must therefore be regulated as radioactive waste under the relevant legislative and regulatory systems overseen by the appropriate authorities. 

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 19 Technology development The evolution of nuclear reactor designs and deployment Introduction Technology development continues with new applications Since the dawn of the nuclear age in the mid-20th century, reactor designs have undergone a profound evolution, shaped by scientific advances, safety imperatives, economic pressures and shifting public attitudes. Early reactors were experimental devices focused on demonstrating the feasibility of nuclear fission for electricity generation. Today, the nuclear industry pursues highly advanced Generation III and IV designs, as well as Small Modular Reactors (SMRs) and even fusion concepts. Operational nuclear plant technologies by region Africa Asia East Europe & Russia North America South America West & Central Europe Total BWR 19 33 8 60 FNR 2 2 GCR 8 8 HTGR 1 1 LWGR 10 10 PHWR 24 17 3 2 46 PWR 2 109 41 63 2 96 313 Total 2 153 53 113 5 114 440 Source: IAEA-PRIS First-generation reactors: From experiment to commercialisation The first nuclear reactors, built in the 1940s and 1950s, were largely research and military oriented. The Chicago Pile-1 (1942), designed by Enrico Fermi’s team, demonstrated the world’s first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction. Shortly afterward, reactors such as the Hanford B Reactor in the US produced plutonium for weapons programmes. Civilian deployment began in earnest in the 1950s. The Obninsk reactor in the Soviet Union (1954) was the first grid-connected nuclear power plant, though it produced only 5MW of electricity. Britain’s Calder Hall (1956) marked the first large- scale plant intended for both electricity generation and plutonium production. These early reactors, often categorised as Generation I, were characterised by bespoke designs, relatively low efficiencies, and limited safety features by today’s standards. Nuclear reactor construction starts and grid connection (reactor Source: IAEA-PRIS PWR technology first developed for submarine technology Generation II reactors: Standardisation and expansion The late 1960s through the 1990s witnessed the rise of Generation II reactors, which formed the backbone of today’s operating fleet. The most prominent design was the Pressurised Water Reactor (PWR), first developed for naval propulsion in the US Navy’s submarine programme. Its compact design, stable behaviour under varying conditions, and closed primary loop made it attractive for commercial deployment. 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 1954 1956 1958 1960 1962 1964 1966 1968 1970 1972 1974 1976 1978 1980 1982 1984 1986 1988 1990 1992 1994 1996 1998 2000 2002 2004 2006 2008 2010 2012 2014 2016 2018 2020 2022 2024 Construction starts Grid connections

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 20 Alongside PWRs, the Boiling Water Reactor (BWR), developed by General Electric, became widespread. Both designs use light water as a coolant and moderator but differ in steam generation: in a BWR, steam is produced directly in the reactor vessel, while in a PWR, steam is generated in secondary loops. Other Generation II technologies included the CANDU (Canada Deuterium Uranium) reactor, using heavy water as moderator and natural uranium as fuel, and the AGR (Advanced Gas- cooled Reactor) in the UK, which used carbon dioxide as coolant and graphite as moderator. The era of Generation II saw rapid expansion: by the mid- 1980s, hundreds of reactors were under construction worldwide, particularly in North America, Europe, Japan, and the Soviet Union. Standardisation efforts sought to reduce costs and improve reliability, but the industry also grappled with significant setbacks. Nuclear accidents have led to further safety features being implemented Impact of accidents on design evolution Two major accidents profoundly influenced reactor design philosophy. 1. Three Mile Island (1979, US): A partial meltdown at a PWR in Pennsylvania exposed deficiencies in operator training, instrumentation, and emergency preparedness. While no deaths occurred, the incident damaged public trust and highlighted the need for redundant safety systems and human-machine interface improvements. 2. Chernobyl (1986, USSR): A catastrophic explosion at an RBMK graphite-moderated reactor in Ukraine underscored the risks of flawed design, inadequate safety culture, and lack of containment. The disaster accelerated international cooperation on nuclear safety and imposed stricter design standards worldwide. Both accidents led to a re-emphasis on containment structures, passive safety features, and robust emergency core-cooling systems. They also spurred the industry to pursue new generations of reactors with enhanced safety margins. Generation III and III+ reactors: Safety and economics Generation III reactors emerged in the 1990s and 2000s as evolutionary improvements over their predecessors. Their goals were enhanced safety, longer operational lifespans, reduced maintenance, and improved economics. Key features included: • Passive safety systems that rely on natural circulation, gravity, or convection, reducing dependence on active pumps and operator intervention. • Standardised designs to streamline licensing and reduce construction costs. • Longer fuel cycles and higher burnup rates, improving fuel utilisation. Examples include the AP1000 (Westinghouse), the EPR (European Pressurised Reactor, Areva/Framatome), and the VVER-1200 (Rosatom). Japan developed the ABWR (Advanced Boiling Water Reactor), which became the first- Generation III design to enter service in 1996. Despite technical advances, deployment has faced significant obstacles. Projects such as Olkiluoto-3 in Finland, Flamanville- 3 in France, and Vogtle-3/4 in the US have experienced severe cost overruns and delays. These challenges highlight the tension between increasingly complex safety demands and the realities of large-scale project management. Reactor types currently under construction (MW/unit) Source: IAEA Generation IV includes a sustainable approach to recycling nuclear waste Generation IV concepts: Toward sustainability and innovation The Generation IV International Forum (GIF), established in 2000, coordinates research into advanced reactor concepts targeting long-term sustainability, safety, and proliferation resistance. Six main designs are under active study: 1. Gas-cooled Fast Reactor (GFR) 2. Lead-cooled Fast Reactor (LFR) 3. Molten Salt Reactor (MSR) 4. Sodium-cooled Fast Reactor (SFR) 5. Supercritical-water-cooled Reactor (SCWR) 6. Very-high-temperature Reactor (VHTR) These designs often aim to close the nuclear fuel cycle by recycling actinides (metallic radioactive elements), reduce long-lived waste, and achieve higher thermal efficiencies. The MSR has attracted renewed attention for its potential flexibility in load-following and hydrogen production. While most Generation IV concepts remain at the research or demonstration stage, their promise lies in combining low- carbon energy with long-term resource sustainability. 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 0 10,000 20,000 30,000 40,000 50,000 60,000 Boiling Light-Water Cooled and Moderated Reactor Fast Breeder Reactor Pressurized Heavy-Water Moderated and Cooled Reactor Pressurized Light-Water Moderated and Cooled Reactor BWR FBR PHWR PWR Total Net Electrical Capacity Number of Reactors

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 21 SMR holds promise in standardisation and capital efficiency Small modular reactors (SMRs): A new paradigm In parallel with Generation IV work, the industry is focusing on SMRs. These reactors, typically producing 50-300MW each, are designed for factory fabrication, modular deployment and scalability. SMRs aim to address many of the economic and logistical barriers of large reactors: • Lower upfront capital costs due to smaller size and modular construction. • Shorter construction times through factory manufacturing. • Flexibility in pairing with renewable energy sources or deployment in remote regions. • Enhanced safety features, often relying on passive systems and underground siting. Projects include NuScale’s VOYGR design in the US, Rolls- Royce’s SMR in the UK, and Russia’s floating Akademik Lomonosov plant. Countries such as Canada and China are also actively pursuing SMR deployment. SMRs may redefine nuclear deployment by enabling incremental capacity additions, more manageable financing, and applications beyond electricity, such as district heating, desalination, or hydrogen production .  The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 22 Nuclear fusion remains a distant prospect, but interesting Nuclear fusion: The long-term vision Parallel to fission development, research in nuclear fusion has long promised virtually limitless clean energy. Fusion seeks to replicate the processes powering the sun, typically by fusing deuterium and tritium at extremely high temperatures. The most visible project is ITER (International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor) in France, a massive international collaboration aiming to demonstrate sustained fusion power by the 2030s. Complementary efforts include stellarator research in Germany (Wendelstein 7-X) and private ventures exploring compact fusion devices. While commercial fusion remains decades away, its pursuit symbolises the nuclear industry’s long-term aspiration for safer, cleaner, and more abundant energy. Deployment trends and regional variation The evolution of designs has unfolded unevenly across regions. • Europe and North America initially led in deployment but have slowed in recent decades due to high costs, regulatory complexity, and public opposition. • Asia has become the centre of new construction. China is aggressively building advanced reactors, including Hualong One and CAP1400 designs, and is exploring both SMRs and Generation IV concepts. • Russia continues to expand exports of VVER reactors, positioning itself as a dominant supplier for global markets. • Emerging economies in the Middle East, Africa, and South America are considering nuclear power to meet rising energy demand and climate targets. These regional patterns reflect broader trends in financing capacity, state support, and societal acceptance. Small modular reactors (SMR) SMRs are in focus, specifically in the US and in Europe Nuclear power has returned to the centre of energy policy debates as governments confront the combined pressures of climate commitments, volatile energy markets, and rapidly rising electricity demand. Within this renewed attention, SMRs are receiving particular interest. For investors, the appeal is straightforward: SMRs promise a version of nuclear power that could be more modular, predictable, and scalable than the large, bespoke gigawatt-scale units that have historically dominated the sector. While traditional nuclear development has often been restrained by concerns about cost overruns, safety incidents, and unresolved waste questions, the landscape is shifting. The Nuclear Energy Agency now tracks a large and growing pipeline of SMR designs, and billions of dollars in venture and corporate capital have been committed to move the most promising concepts towards demonstration and eventual commercial operation. The core argument behind SMRs is that they can integrate more flexibly with wind and solar, be built and financed in smaller increments, and offer investors a pathway to replicate standardised plants across multiple sites. These characteristics, if demonstrated, could position SMRs as a viable building block of the energy transition, particularly in markets where grid constraints, industrial heat demand, or policy frameworks call for firm, low-carbon power that complements renewables. A changing capacity outlook to 2050 Looking ahead to 2050, nuclear capacity is expected to grow meaningfully as electrification accelerates across transport, buildings, digital infrastructure and industry. Much of the capacity slated for the 2020s and early 2030s remains tied to traditional large reactors that are already in construction or advanced planning, reflecting long project cycles. But as governments reassess their decarbonisation pathways, new nuclear options, including SMRs, are beginning to feature more prominently. Asian nuclear capacity growth will form the centre of nuclear capacity growth while SMRs will play a more prominent role in EU and US plans Projections suggest global nuclear capacity could reach roughly 800GW by 2050, with Asia leading the expansion. Importantly, more than a third of that potential sit in “unannounced” or early-concept stages, projects that are not yet tied to a specific design. If SMR technologies reach regulatory and supply-chain maturity early in the 2030s, a portion of these future builds could be redirected towards modular units. For investors, this means that SMRs may become a credible competitor for capital allocation in the next decade, provided early demonstrations prove deliverable on time and within budget. Global distribution of SMR development efforts Current development patterns offer some insight into where commercial activity may first materialise. Based on Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA) tracking, nearly half of all active SMR technologies originate in North America, where public-private models and a strong innovation ecosystem have driven early momentum. Europe accounts for nearly one-third of the tracked designs, reflecting both decarbonisation ambitions and regional energy-security concerns. Asia holds a smaller but nonetheless important share, with China in particular advancing both conventional and next-generation concepts under state-led programmes. Russia also continues to invest in modular reactors for remote locations and maritime applications. Thus far, water-cooled SMRs, those closely related to today’s light-water reactors, have progressed most quickly, leveraging existing regulatory familiarity and supply-chain capability. However, incremental licensing developments in Generation IV designs, such as molten-salt, liquid-metal, and high-temperature gas-cooled reactors, indicate that advanced concepts are beginning to gain traction in select countries. A handful of gas-cooled and molten-salt projects have reached critical licensing milestones, signalling early regulatory learning in these novel systems. 

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 23 SMR activity, 2025 (units) Source: IAEA A broad portfolio of projects is developing in parallel Assessing commercial readiness across projects Twelve projects stand out with the strongest overall momentum. Russia dominates this group, driven by state- supported investment and tightly integrated fuel supply and manufacturing capabilities. China and the US also feature prominently, each with several high-performing initiatives that highlight a mix of public leadership and private-sector innovation. Canada, Romania, Argentina and Uzbekistan are likewise represented among the most competitive efforts. A clear distinction is evident in development models: Russian and Chinese projects tend to advance through centrally coordinated state programmes, while those in the US are more often propelled by private capital, strategic partnerships, and supportive policy frameworks. Currently, large nuclear plants dominate but there is an increasing focus on SMRs First movers and near-term candidates Although conventional nuclear plants still dominate global construction pipelines, a handful of SMRs have already crossed the threshold into operation. Russia operates a floating nuclear power plant based on twin 35MW pressurised-water modules that have delivered power since 2019. In China, the HTR-PM, a high-temperature, pebble-bed gas-cooled design, entered commercial service in late 2023. Its ability to reach high outlet temperatures suggests long-term potential not only for electricity but also for industrial heat applications. Japan’s small reactor, though not a commercial SMR in the modern sense, demonstrates long-running operational experience in compact reactor systems. Risk sharing with governments central to developing new technologies In North America, the real test will come over the next several years. The US hosts the world’s largest pipeline of SMR developers, ranging from light-water concepts to advanced fuels and coolants. The federal government has begun reshaping regulatory and financing structures to support first-of-a-kind deployments, recognising that private investors alone cannot shoulder demonstration risk. Sponsor type by market Source: IAEA, IEA Canada is also moving decisively. Ontario has granted approvals for the nation’s first SMR at the Darlington site, using GE Hitachi’s BWRX-300 design. With a construction license secured and an operational target in sight, Darlington is widely viewed as one of the most credible candidates to deliver a replicable model in the near term. GE Hitachi has expanded its footprint with an agreement in Poland that contemplates up to 24 SMR units, an indication of growing demand among European industrial players seeking dependable, low-carbon power. In the US, the BWRX-300 and Kairos Power’s Hermes molten-salt reactor have attracted great attention. The former is advancing through the federal licensing process, with early-2030s operation envisioned at the Tennessee Valley Authority’s Clinch River site. The latter is building its low-power demonstration reactor, marking the first US authorisation for a Generation IV technology in more than half a century. NuScale, one of the earliest and best-known SMR developers, continues to promote its 77MW module, designed to be deployed in multi-unit configurations. Its partnership-based deployments in Tennessee and Romania, including a six-module 462MW project in Doicești, highlight the role of international cooperation and anchor-customer commitments in advancing SMRs towards final investment decisions. In Asia, China’s ACP100, a 125MW integrated pressurised- water design, is nearing completion after entering construction in 2021. It benefits from state backing, strong domestic supply chains, and a track record of rapid execution across multiple reactor types. Russia, through Rosatom, continues to push forward with floating RITM-200S reactors and other maritime concepts designed for remote or harsh environments. 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 US Canada UK Russia Poland China Finland Sweden France Romania Philippines Ukraine South Korea Uzbekistan Guinea Slovakia Indonesia Rwanda Netherlands Myanmar Saudi Arabia Argentina Japan Czechia 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100% 120% Advanced economies Russia China Other EMDE State-owned enterprises Non-government companies

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 24 SMRs to be considered with final investment decision in 2029-30 Europe is also gaining momentum. Sweden’s Ringhals programme, with Vattenfall evaluating GE Hitachi and Rolls-Royce SMR designs, could become the first Nordic deployment by the mid-2030s. The UK is advancing Rolls-Royce’s 470MW design through its national assessment process and has committed billions in government support. Interest from Poland, Czechia, and Sweden further underscores the region’s appetite for SMRs as part of industrial and grid-transition strategies. The cost challenge Can SMRs deliver competitive, repeatable costs? First-of-a-kind units almost always face cost premiums, and the true financial promise of SMRs rests on achieving economies of series, standardised units, replicated across multiple sites, produced with factory-built modules, and built by a trained labour force that gains efficiency with every unit. Investors will require evidence that schedule discipline and cost predictability are attainable, not merely aspirational. Learning curve and standardisation to support cost decreases in construction cost, construction time and supply chain Two trends strengthen the investment case. Large technology companies, particularly those operating energy-intensive data centres, increasingly seek 24/7 carbon-free electricity and are prepared to sign long-term offtake agreements with nuclear developers. Second, governments are modernising incentives, through tax credits, risk-sharing mechanisms, regulated revenue models or direct funding, to reduce the cost of capital and attract private investment. SMR construction cost (USD/kW) Source: IEA (2025) Nuclear power is suited to generate heat, and SMR could play a role in heating Opportunities and remaining uncertainties From an investor’s perspective, the potential upside is significant. SMRs offer a pathway to modular, scalable, low-carbon power that can be tailored to industrial clusters, remote grids, or brownfield sites where large reactors would be impractical. Advanced SMRs can supply high-temperature heat, enabling new revenue opportunities in hydrogen, synthetic fuels, district heating, and industrial processing. Their ability to ramp up output could complement variable renewable energy, enhancing system-level reliability. However, key uncertainties remain. Manufacturing capacity for reactor modules and components must expand. Licensing frameworks need to evolve to accommodate standardised designs and multi-site deployment. Fuel supply chains, especially for advanced fuels such as HALEU, must scale up. Above all, SMR developers must show that a series-based model can substitute for the economies of scale that supported traditional large reactors. Without repeatable delivery at predictable costs, investors will remain cautious. Signals to watch in the coming years For capital providers evaluating SMR participation, several milestones will clarify the technology’s trajectory: • First-of-a-kind SMRs that meet cost and scheduling expectations, including Darlington’s BWRX-300, early Kairos Hermes achievements, China’s ACP100, and Europe’s initial deployments. • Progress towards regulatory standardisation and multi-unit licensing frameworks. • Fuel-cycle investments that ensure secure supply for advanced SMRs. • Corporate off-take agreements from data centres, industrial customers, and utilities. • Policy and revenue structures, such as contracts for difference or regulated asset models, that reduce risk and lower financing costs. If these elements align, SMRs could transition from isolated demonstrations to a recognised, financeable asset class, complementing renewables and storage while strengthening energy security. SMRs could play an important role in providing stable baseload at lower risk Bottom line SMRs will not transform the energy system on their own, but they have a credible pathway to become a meaningful pillar of the low-carbon portfolio. The early signs – operational units in Russia and China, licensing progress across multiple countries, growing corporate interest, and advancing projects in North America and Europe – suggest the sector is maturing. The decisive test now is execution. Should the first generation of commercial SMRs deliver on time and budget, the technology could evolve into a scalable, exportable, and investable clean-energy solution throughout the 2030s and 2040s. If not, expectations may reset once again. The next several years will determine how much of the promise translates into bankable reality. 0 1,000 2,000 3,000 4,000 5,000 6,000 7,000 US & EU China India Base Case High Case

From the rudimentary Generation I prototype to today’s advanced designs, each stage has sought to improve safety, efficiency, and reliability. Accidents like Three Mile Island and Chernobyl profoundly shifted design priorities, while climate change and the decarbonisation agenda now drive renewed interest in nuclear deployment. The industry stands at a crossroads: large Generation III+ reactors face economic headwinds, but SMRs and Generation IV concepts hold promises for a more sustainable, flexible, and globally deployable future. The continuing evolution of nuclear reactor designs will remain central to the debate over how best to meet the world’s growing energy needs while minimising environmental impact. Levelised cost of energy (LCOE) Clean power became markedly cheaper from 2024 onwards, as easing supply-chain pressures and improving financing conditions pushed costs to new lows. The benchmark LCOE fell to roughly USD 36/MWh for fixed-tilt solar and USD 38/MWh for onshore wind, while four-hour standalone battery storage dropped sharply to around USD 104/MWh. This decline in storage costs was driven by battery pack prices falling to about USD 115/kWh, a 20% reduction in a single year and more than 90% below 2010 levels. These trends reflect long-established learning curves, particularly in solar, where costs have historically fallen by close to 28% with each doubling of cumulative capacity, and they also mirror the return of more favourable capital market conditions. Interest rates and subsequently return requirements have a significant impact on the viability and competitive outlook for a nuclear plant, or for that matter most long-term infrastructure investments. Interest rates affect the overall LCOE of a project significantly, especially if there are delays LCOE, 2025 (EUR/MWh) Source: Lazard, BNEF Mass production reduces costs Manufacturing scale, especially in mainland China, remains the single most powerful force shaping global cost dynamics. Persistent oversupply pushed mainstream solar module prices to around USD 0.10/W by the end of 2024, close to or even below production cost. As a result, global fixed-tilt solar costs fell by more than 20% in one year, with little sign that oversupply will abate in the near term. Markets that can source directly or indirectly from Chinese supply chains continue to benefit most. Wind power tells a more complex story. Within China, intense competition drove turbine prices down and cut onshore wind costs significantly, but outside China higher turbine prices lifted average wind LCOEs despite the global benchmark still falling slightly. Offshore wind costs rose worldwide as expensive turbines and tight installation markets offset financing improvements. These divergences have created stark regional differences: Chinese benchmark costs for solar, wind, storage and offshore wind are often 10-60% lower than those elsewhere, while outside China average costs are roughly double. The main exception is gas-fired generation, where China lacks access to abundant low-cost fuel. Fossil fuels-based generation is subject to greater volatility for fuel and is exposed to carbon pricing Thermal generation costs moved in a different direction. As fuel price assumptions eased from the peaks of 2022, coal- and gas-fired LCOEs fell by around 12%. Hydrogen-fired power, however, became substantially more expensive, rising to roughly USD 425/MWh as estimates for green hydrogen costs were revised upward. In many markets that price carbon, combined-cycle gas turbines regain a short-run dispatch advantage over coal, yet their lifetime economics remain structurally worse than before the energy crisis. In Europe, new gas plants frequently require prices well above EUR 100/MWh to be viable, even before blending with hydrogen, reinforcing the view that new gas capacity is increasingly a system insurance product rather than a competitive energy source.  The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 26 decarbonisation strategies now matter more for bankability than headline LCOEs. The easing of financing conditions has been pivotal in accelerating cost declines. As inflation subsided and rate-cut cycles began in 2024, the weighted average cost of capital for new solar projects fell materially. In China, state-backed developers secured extremely cheap debt, pushing capital costs down even further, while coal projects faced rising financing costs as lenders priced in stranded-asset risks. Expectations of further monetary easing in 2025 suggest additional reductions in clean energy LCOEs, particularly where falling debt costs combine with maturing technology risk. This reinforces a central lesson of recent years: for most power technologies, the cost of capital is now the dominant swing factor, and differences in financing terms increasingly explain the dispersion seen in auctions and corporate power-purchase agreements. Estimated cost/kW (USD) Source: Lazard (2025) Renewables, with or without various forms of storage attached, make up the bulk of new generation capacity Across most of the world, renewables now dominate new-build economics. Wind or solar is the cheapest source of new bulk power in systems representing roughly two-thirds of global electricity demand, and in many markets new renewables are already cheaper than operating existing coal or gas plants. India illustrates this shift clearly, where rapid solar cost declines mean new utility-scale PV can undercut the running costs of typical coal units. While some fossil assets remain competitive in specific contexts, pairing renewables with four-hour storage is increasingly decisive, allowing output to be shifted into higher-priced hours and reducing curtailment risk. In several advanced markets, combined wind-plus-storage projects are already cheaper than existing coal or gas generation. The United Kingdom provides a clear example of how policy design can convert technology learning into bankable outcomes. After offshore wind auctions failed in 2023 due to unrealistic price caps, revised frameworks with higher caps and longer contracts restored investor confidence. Subsequent auctions secured record volumes of offshore wind at prices well below new gas generation, illustrating that stable, long-tenor revenue arrangements can materially lower financing costs and restore competitiveness, even for capital-intensive technologies. The LCOE does not consider system value in a volatile price environment As renewable penetration rises, however, the distinction between LCOE and system value becomes critical. Falling wholesale prices and a growing number of negative-price hours in parts of Europe highlight that flexibility, storage, interconnection and market design increasingly determine realised revenues. Projects with contracted offtake, hybrid configurations or locational advantages command lower required returns, while merchant projects exposed solely to spot markets face rising cannibalisation risks. Nuclear power remains sharply bifurcated. Extending the life of existing reactors is often one of the cheapest sources of low-carbon electricity, typically below EUR 40/MWh, because it is capital-light and benefits from sunk infrastructure costs. New nuclear builds, by contrast, carry substantial cost and schedule risks. Their economics are highly sensitive to financing and delivery performance, with long construction periods magnifying interest during construction. In many European and North American contexts, first-of-a-kind nuclear projects still produce electricity at multiples of renewable costs, even when compared with gas equipped with carbon capture. Implementation must be backed by policies and frameworks Overall, the evidence points to a durable reshaping of global power-sector economics. Solar, wind and storage are cheaper than ever and set to become cheaper still, with new renewables increasingly outperforming both new fossil generation and the operating costs of existing plants, particularly when combined with storage. Yet low theoretical costs only translate into built projects where policy, market design and financing frameworks are aligned. For investors and lenders, the message is: renewables are the default choice for new capacity, storage is becoming essential for value capture, gas increasingly relies on capacity revenues, and nuclear competitiveness hinges on life extension and explicit risk-sharing models rather than greenfield construction alone. Delays lead to cost overruns The future viability of the nuclear sector hinges largely on whether both large reactors and the emerging generation of small modular reactors can be delivered on schedule and within their projected budgets. Because nuclear power is dominated by capital and financing costs, any delay or cost overrun can sharply erode its economic appeal. When investors perceive construction risks as high, the technology quickly becomes less competitive compared with other options. 0 2,000 4,000 6,000 8,000 10,000 12,000 14,000 16,000 USA (Vogtle) France (Flamanville low) France (Flamanville high) England (Hinkley C original) England (Hinkley C latest) Lazard LCOE Nuclear (low est.)  The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 27 Construction time, number of reactors, 1991-2024 Source: IAEA-PRIS Nuclear energy’s competitiveness ultimately depends not only on how much it costs to build and operate reactors, but also on how these costs compare with alternative sources of power. For technologies that provide similar services, particularly dispatchable, bulk electricity generation, the LCOE is the standard benchmark. This metric captures all major expenses over a plant’s lifetime, from construction and maintenance to fuel, carbon pricing and eventual decommissioning. Construction time (years) Source: WNISR, 2025 While nuclear plants require large upfront investments, they benefit from relatively low fuel costs and typically achieve high-capacity factors, often exceeding 75%. These features help keep their lifetime generating costs lower than might otherwise be expected for such capital-heavy infrastructure. Construction cost and cost of capital are central to being able to develop competitive nuclear power Looking ahead to 2040, projected LCOEs vary widely by region, largely depending on construction costs and the cost of financing. In China, estimates fall between USD 50-70/MWh, depending on borrowing costs. In the US and European Union, higher construction expenditures push projected LCOEs into a higher range: roughly USD 60-100/MWh in the US and USD 75- 110/MWh in Europe. By contrast, extending the operational life of existing reactors remains significantly cheaper than building new ones, suggesting that lifetime extensions will continue to play an important role wherever feasible. Cost (USD/kW) Source: Lazard (2025 est) Construction cost is central to the viability of new nuclear plants. Although nuclear energy can provide decades of stable, low‑carbon electricity with relatively low operating costs, the enormous upfront investment required to build a plant is what ultimately determines whether projects move forward, attract financing, and remain competitive with alternatives such as renewables, gas, or storage‑based systems. In other words, nuclear economics are shaped far more by the capital cost, and the cost of capital, than by fuel or operational expenses. This is why governments and developers focus so intently on financing models, risk‑sharing mechanisms, and project delivery discipline: construction cost is the make‑or‑break variable. Delays have a significant impact on the cost of power The sensitivity to construction cost arises from nuclear power’s long development timelines and the fact that capital must be tied up for many years before a plant begins earning revenue. Every year of delay compounds the financial burden, pushing up interest during construction and eroding the economic case. This is starkly illustrated by large Western nuclear projects in recent decades. Plants such as Olkiluoto 3 in Finland or Flamanville 3 in France saw costs multiply to several times their original estimates, largely because schedules stretched far beyond what was planned. Even in the UK, Hinkley Point C’s cost inflation and repeated delays have forced a complete re- evaluation of the financing model for Sizewell C. These cases show how nuclear construction risk translates almost directly into financial risk, and why investors demand predictable revenue arrangements before committing capital. 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 1991-1995 1996-2000 2001-2005 2006-2010 2011-2015 2016-2020 2021-2024 Number of reactors Construction time (mths) 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 Belarusian -2 Fangchenggang 3 Fangchenggang 4 Fuqing - 6 Hongyanhe - 6 Shidao Bay 1-2 Shidaowan Guohe One-1 Zhangzhou 1 Olkiluoto-3 Flamanville-3 Kakrapar-4 Kanupp-3 Mochove-3 Shin-Hanul 1 Shin-Hanul 2 Barakh-3 Barakh-4 Vogtle 3 Vogtle 4 Expected construction time Delay Uncertainty Suspension 0 2,000 4,000 6,000 8,000 10,000 12,000 14,000 16,000 USA (Vogtle) France (Flamanville low) France (Flamanville high) England (Hinkley C original) England (Hinkley C latest) Lazard LCOE Nuclear (low est.)  The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 28 Development timeline Source: IAEA, WNISR Construction costs are driven by a variety of factors What drives construction costs is a complex interplay of technical, regulatory, industrial, and political factors. One of the most important is project standardisation. When a nuclear fleet is built using a repeatable design – France’s expansion in the 1970s and 80s is the classic example – learning curves, established supply chains, and experienced construction teams drive down both cost and risk. Conversely, one‑off or first‑of‑a‑kind designs tend to suffer from design changes, integration challenges, and quality‑assurance issues that cascade into delays. The global resurgence of small modular reactors is, in part, an attempt to recreate the economies of replication that earlier nuclear programmes enjoyed. Delays in recent nuclear plants (years) Source: WNISR, IAEA (2025) A predictable regulatory environment creates opportunities for a lower construction cost Regulatory environments also influence cost significantly. While stringent safety oversight is non‑negotiable, fragmented or unpredictable licensing processes can slow projects, require redesigns, or force developers to carry large contingencies. Countries with more coordinated regulatory frameworks, such as South Korea during its rapid nuclear build‑out, have historically delivered lower construction costs. In contrast, environments where regulators, utilities and political authorities are misaligned tend to experience prolonged permitting cycles and shifting compliance requirements. Supply‑chain maturity is another major determinant. Modern nuclear construction requires highly specialised manufacturing capability, from heavy forgings to reactor coolant pumps to control systems. If the domestic supply chain has atrophied, as happened in Europe after decades of limited new build, countries must either rebuild industrial capacity or rely on imported components, both of which increase cost and risk. Workforce capability is part of this: nuclear construction requires a deep reservoir of civil engineering, welding, materials testing, and quality‑control expertise, and losing these skills between build cycles leads to rising costs when new programmes eventually restart. Government measures or support can affect financing conditions Financing conditions interact tightly with construction risk. Since nuclear projects are capital‑intensive, the cost of borrowing, driven by country risk, political confidence and policy clarity, can represent a large share of the total cost. For this reason, governments increasingly play a direct role in financing, through state loans, guarantees, or revenue‑stabilisation schemes. Lowering the cost of capital can cut the LCOE by tens of percent, even if the physical construction cost remains unchanged. Conversely, high financing costs can render otherwise technically sound projects unviable. Political stability and a predictable outlook for energy policies is central for nuclear power Policy stability plays its own quiet but powerful role. Nuclear power requires decisions that span multiple political cycles. Uncertain future regulation, shifting energy strategies, or political opposition can raise perceived risk and push financing costs upward. Germany’s abrupt post‑Fukushima policy reversal is often cited as an example of how political risk affects the investment climate. Countries attempting new build today therefore focus on creating long‑horizon frameworks that give investors’ confidence the rules will not shift mid‑construction. Beyond economics, public acceptance can influence construction cost by shaping siting decisions, regulatory scrutiny and litigation risk. Projects that face prolonged opposition often encounter delays, redesigns, or costly mitigation measures. Conversely, where there is strong social license, permitting tends to proceed more predictably, which reduces the overall risk premium attached to the project. All these factors combine into one overarching conclusion: construction cost is not just a technical matter but a systemic one. Delivering cost‑competitive nuclear power requires stable policy, a mature supply chain, well‑structured financing, and designs that can be repeated rather than reinvented. When these pieces align, construction costs fall and nuclear becomes a powerful long‑term asset. When they do not, even the best engineering cannot overcome the financial drag of delays and uncertainty 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 1991-1995 1996-2000 2001-2005 2006-2010 2011-2015 2016-2020 2021-2024 Number of reactors Construction time (mths) 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 Belarusian -2 Fangchenggang 3 Fangchenggang 4 Fuqing - 6 Hongyanhe - 6 Shidao Bay 1-2 Shidaowan Guohe One-1 Zhangzhou 1 Olkiluoto-3 Flamanville-3 Kakrapar-4 Kanupp-3 Mochove-3 Shin-Hanul 1 Shin-Hanul 2 Barakh-3 Barakh-4 Vogtle 3 Vogtle 4 Expected construction time Delay Uncertainty Suspension

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 29 Nuclear as a sustainable investment Nuclear power is now included in the EU taxonomy Nuclear power and the EU taxonomy The inclusion of nuclear energy in the European Union’s sustainable finance taxonomy marks one of the most consequential, and controversial, policy debates of the energy transition. It reflects Europe’s attempt to balance scientific assessment, political compromise, and market realities as it defines what can be labelled “green” for investors. The outcome has not made nuclear power fully “green” in the same way as renewables, but it has provided a carefully framed path for recognising nuclear as a legitimate, transitional tool in the EU’s decarbonisation strategy. Avoided CO2 (Gt) due to nuclear power generation Source: IAEA (2023) Nuclear power has varying degree of support throughout Europe From exclusion to cautious inclusion When the EU Taxonomy Regulation was adopted in 2020, its goal was to guide investors by identifying which activities substantially contribute to climate and environmental objectives. Nuclear power was initially excluded pending further study, as member states were sharply divided: France and several Eastern European countries were strongly in favour, while Germany, Austria, and Luxembourg opposed its inclusion on environmental and safety grounds. To resolve the impasse, the European Commission requested an independent scientific review. The Joint Research Centre (JRC), the Commission’s in-house science body, was tasked with assessing whether nuclear could meet the taxonomy’s “do no significant harm” (DNSH) principle, particularly regarding waste management and long-term environmental safety. In 2021, the JRC concluded that nuclear energy, when managed under strict EU safety and waste rules, did not cause more significant harm than other low-carbon technologies. Two additional expert groups, the Euratom Article 31 group and the Commission’s Scientific Committee on Health, Environmental and Emerging Risks (SCHEER), broadly endorsed the JRC’s findings. The Complementary Climate Delegated Act Building on this evidence, the Commission proposed the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (CDA) in February 2022, which added certain nuclear (and natural gas) activities to the taxonomy under the label of “transitional activities.” This meant that nuclear power could be considered sustainable if it contributed to climate mitigation and did not undermine other environmental goals, but it would be subject to strict technical screening criteria and enhanced disclosure rules. New technologies and lifetime extensions are included in the EU taxonomy The act defined three eligible categories of nuclear activity: • New Generation III+ reactors with construction permits granted before 2045. • Lifetime extensions of existing reactors, if approved before 2040. • Research and development of advanced technologies, such as Generation IV and small modular reactors (SMRs). To qualify, projects must demonstrate the highest safety standards, have robust waste-management plans, and provide a credible path toward operational disposal facilities for radioactive waste. Exporting nuclear waste for disposal outside the EU is prohibited. Moreover, companies involved must publicly disclose how their nuclear activities meet these requirements, allowing investors to make informed choices. After heated debate, the European Parliament voted in July 2022 not to block the CDA, effectively approving the inclusion of nuclear in the taxonomy. The act entered into force on 1 January 2023, with a review scheduled every three years to ensure that criteria remain aligned with technological and environmental developments. Legal and political outcomes The inclusion of nuclear immediately faced legal challenges. Austria and Luxembourg brought an action before the EU General Court, arguing that nuclear power cannot be considered environmentally sustainable. However, in September 2025, the Court dismissed the challenge, ruling that the Commission had acted within its powers and that the inclusion of nuclear as a transitional activity was legally justified. This decision effectively settled years of regulatory uncertainty and confirmed that nuclear energy could remain part of the EU’s sustainable finance framework, at least under its current conditions. Acceptance of nuclear power differs from country to country Politically, the taxonomy debate underscored Europe’s divide over nuclear energy. France, Finland, the Czech Republic, and several Eastern European member states viewed the outcome as a pragmatic recognition of nuclear’s role in achieving net- zero targets and energy security. In contrast, Austria and Germany reaffirmed their opposition, maintaining domestic bans and calling for stronger safeguards in future revisions. 0 10 20 30 40 50 60 70 80 European Union United States Japan Korea Canada Other advanced economies Developing economies

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 30 Effects on policy, investment, and permitting Although the taxonomy is primarily a financial classification system, its ripple effects have been substantial. The inclusion of nuclear has given political and regulatory legitimacy to national programmes aiming at expanding or extending nuclear capacity, especially in countries that rely on it for low-carbon baseload generation. France, for instance, cited the taxonomy decision as supporting its plans for new reactors and its small modular reactor programme, while other member states, such as Poland, the Czech Republic, and Sweden, have referenced taxonomy alignment in justifying their nuclear strategies. Inclusion in the EU taxonomy reduces regulatory risk for nuclear power From an investment perspective, nuclear’s taxonomy status has reduced perceived regulatory risk and clarified disclosure rules for investors. It enables nuclear projects to be included in taxonomy-aligned investment portfolios and potentially in EU Green Bonds, provided they meet all technical criteria and transparency obligations. While the taxonomy does not compel investors to finance nuclear projects, it removes the formal barrier that excluded them from “sustainable” investment categories, helping utilities and developers access a broader range of capital. Financial markets have responded with cautious optimism. Companies linked to advanced nuclear technologies, such as Rolls-Royce SMR, GE Vernova (through GE-Hitachi), and NuScale, have seen rising investor interest, reflecting a belief that policy signals and new financing mechanisms could open the door to nuclear innovation. There is a time limit for extension and new construction of nuclear power in the taxonomy The taxonomy has also had practical effects on permitting and project design. Developers now align their environmental and technical documentation with taxonomy criteria, particularly on waste management, safety, and financing structure, to demonstrate eligibility and attract green capital. The time- bound conditions (permits by 2040 for extensions, 2045 for new builds) create planning pressure on member states to make decisions within this window. While the taxonomy does not directly shorten permitting times, it has raised the quality and transparency of submissions, with more emphasis on lifecycle assessments and long-term waste solutions. Broader implications The EU’s decision ultimately reframed nuclear’s role in the clean-energy transition. By recognising it as a transitional technology rather than a permanent pillar, the taxonomy strikes a delicate balance between scientific evidence, political realism, and investor demand for clarity. The 2025 court ruling has provided regulatory stability, allowing member states and companies to plan nuclear investments with greater confidence. Still, inclusion in the taxonomy does not resolve nuclear’s fundamental challenges, high capital costs, long construction times, and waste management, but it places the technology on a more even footing with renewables in EU finance law. In practice, the taxonomy has opened the door for nuclear projects to compete for sustainable finance, reinforced its policy credibility, and signalled to investors that nuclear can play a legitimate, albeit conditional, role in Europe’s path to net zero. In essence, the EU taxonomy has not declared nuclear power “green,” but it has acknowledged its importance as a transitional, low-carbon technology under strict environmental and safety safeguards. This carefully balanced decision has strengthened investor confidence, clarified rules for project developers, and given member states a clearer framework for integrating nuclear power into their long-term decarbonisation strategies, while keeping the door open for future revisions as technology and policy evolve. The fuel supply chain The global nuclear-fuel supply chain has become one of the most strategically sensitive parts of the energy system, and its structure today reflects both decades-old industrial geography and the intense geopolitical pressures that have reshaped fuel flows since 2022. At its core, the chain moves uranium from mined ore through refining and conversion into uranium hexafluoride, then through enrichment to raise its fissile content, and finally into fabricated fuel assemblies. Each step requires specialised facilities that exist in only a handful of countries, which is why the supply chain remains vulnerable to bottlenecks and external shocks. Conversion capacity, for example, is concentrated in Canada, France, the US, China, and Russia, and global output has recently tightened as Western converters report being sold out well into the next decade. Honeywell and ConverDyn’s Metropolis Works facility in the US, the country’s only uranium-hexafluoride conversion plant, restarted after years offline but is already fully committed through 2028, highlighting how limited Western conversion availability has become. Nuclear fuel processing and sourcing has been affected by geopolitics Mining itself has shifted towards an even more dominant role for Kazakhstan. The country has long been the world’s largest uranium producer, supplying more than 40% of global primary output in 2024, and its national champion Kazatomprom expects production to have risen further to roughly 25,000- 26,500 tonnes in 2025. Although Kazakhstan’s resource base is vast, the country has repeatedly emphasised supply discipline, signalling that market conditions do not yet justify a full return to previously higher extraction levels. This restraint, combined with long project lead times for new mines elsewhere, underscores why analysts expect potential structural supply deficits to emerge in the 2030s. Similar patterns play out in Canada and Australia, which remain major suppliers but are unable to match Kazakhstan’s large in-situ recovery-based (as opposed to conventional open mining) 

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 31 output. Utilities’ procurement data from the US shows just how globally integrated the uranium trade is: in 2024, deliveries to US reactors were dominated by Canada (36%), Kazakhstan (24%), and Australia (17%), with smaller shares from Uzbekistan, Namibia, Russia, and domestic US producers. Uranium to the EU by source (tonnes) Source: WNISR Once uranium is mined and refined, conversion and enrichment become the true chokepoints. Russia’s Rosatom has historically commanded a very large share of global conversion and enrichment services, accounting in 2022 for roughly 38% of conversion capacity and 46% of enrichment capacity across world markets. Europe’s dependence is particularly pronounced, as Russian entities previously supplied more than a fifth of the EU’s conversion needs and nearly a third of its enrichment services. Despite the sweeping sanctions imposed on other areas of the Russian economy, nuclear fuel has been carved out of most headline restrictions because European utilities still rely heavily on these services. Even so, the political and reputational risks of this dependency have become untenable, prompting the EU to stockpile fuel and accelerate diversification efforts. The US has taken a more forceful route. In 2024 it adopted legislation banning imports of Russian enriched uranium, subject to limited waivers that expire entirely in 2028. As a result, TENEX, the Russian enrichment exporter, issued force-majeure notices to US customers in 2024, warning that utilities would need government waivers to maintain deliveries. Washington’s approach has created uncertainty in short-term supply but also catalysed major federal investment in reconstructing domestic enrichment capability. The Department of Energy (DOE) has committed USD 2.7bn to establish both LEU and high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) capacity, awarding long-term task orders in early 2026 to firms including Centrus Energy, General Matter, and Orano. Centrus reached an important milestone in June 2025 by delivering 900 kilograms of HALEU to the DOE, the first meaningful commercial-scale production of HALEU in the US, underlining how far the country has come after decades without indigenous enrichment. At the same time, Western enrichers are gradually expanding. Urenco has announced capacity additions across its US, Dutch, and German sites, including LEU+ (up to 10% U-235) capability that provides a stepping stone towards HALEU and advanced reactor fuels. Orano is also enlarging its footprint, while the EU has launched multiple research and industrial programmes, such as APIS and SAVE, and related initiatives to accelerate the provision of European-made fuel for VVER reactors in Central and Eastern Europe. These efforts were a direct response to the geopolitical shock of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, which revealed that more than 30 reactors in the EU and Ukraine relied on Russian fuel designs. Some countries, such as Czechia, have already begun receiving Westinghouse-fabricated VVER-compatible fuel assemblies, a major step towards ending the reliance on Russian supplier TVEL. The diversification process is significant for credit analysis because it reduces single-supplier concentration risk but comes with transition challenges, including licensing requirements, first-core testing, and operational adjustments that can introduce timing and inventory uncertainties. Source of uranium by origin (tonnes) Source: EuroAtom Agency Laser-based technology as an emerging technology of enriching Beyond centrifuge technology, the fuel sector is also witnessing the emergence of laser-based enrichment. Global Laser Enrichment (GLE), a joint venture between Silex Systems and Cameco, completed a major demonstration of its SILEX laser-enrichment process in 2025 and advanced its licence application with the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission. The technology promises greater efficiency and the ability to re-enrich depleted uranium tails, an enormous, underutilised resource, thereby expanding Western fuel independence. While commercial viability is still several years away, laser enrichment represents a potential structural shift in enrichment economics and supply security over the long term. All these dynamics have intersected with a pronounced tightening in the uranium, conversion, and enrichment markets. Conversion capacity is particularly constrained, with Western suppliers indicating that little or no uncommitted conversion output will be available before 2029-31, and spot conversion prices have risen accordingly. Uranium spot prices, meanwhile, have fluctuated between roughly the mid-USD 60s and mid-USD 80s per pound through 2024-25, buoyed by investment funds accumulating physical material and by robust long-term contracting from utilities. In the US, utilities paid an average of USD 52.71 per pound for delivered uranium in 2024, with long-term contracts dominating purchasing behaviour, an important stabilising factor for both cash-flow planning and credit risk. Taken together, the world’s nuclear-fuel supply chain is undergoing a managed but still delicate transformation. Western governments are attempting to rebuild capabilities that had atrophied over decades while utilities hedge against delivery risks by diversifying their supply books, increasing inventories, and locking in multi-cycle procurement agreements. From a credit-analysis perspective, this environment elevates the importance of counterparty quality and contract structure. Producers and enrichers with long backlogs, Western policy support, and diversified customer bases are generally positioned relatively more strongly, especially as conversion and enrichment remain seller-advantaged markets. Utilities face rising procurement costs and greater working-capital needs, but many benefit from regulated cost-recovery mechanisms, long-term fuel inventories, and a shifting supplier landscape that promises improved energy security once the ongoing expansion cycle fully matures. Fuel suppliers EU 27+ UK Source: EuroAtom Agency Decommissioning and waste management The residues of the nuclear power generation process require long-term commitment Decommissioning is a central issue for the nuclear industry, requiring the safe handling of radioactive materials. By the end of 2023, 210 reactors worldwide had been permanently shut down, but only 23 were fully decommissioned. Over two-thirds of these reactors are in just five countries: the US, the UK, Germany, Japan, and France. Despite growing experience, global decommissioning remains limited, making future cost estimates uncertain. Decommissioning makes up a significant part of the lifetime cost of a nuclear plant For newly built plants, decommissioning typically represents about 15% of total lifetime investment, equating to roughly 1% of electricity costs when funds are accumulated during operation. Most countries legally require utilities to set aside these funds, with regulators approving both the mechanisms and amounts. Reactor shutdowns by country and installed capacity (MW) Source: IAEA (2025) Safe disposal of spent fuel and radioactive waste remains essential to public trust in nuclear energy. Several countries are advancing deep geological repositories for long-term storage: • Finland: Posiva applied in 2022 for an operating licence for its Olkiluoto disposal facility. Finland’s Onkalo spent nuclear fuel repository is the world’s first fully realised deep geological storage facility for high-level radioactive waste. Situated on Olkiluoto Island in the municipality of Eurajoki, it is designed to isolate spent nuclear fuel roughly 450 metres below the surface in exceptionally old and stable crystalline bedrock. The facility relies on the KBS-3 method, in which the waste is sealed inside copper canisters and surrounded by bentonite clay, creating multiple protective barriers intended to prevent water intrusion and ensure containment for up to 100,000 years. • Sweden: The SFR (short-lived radioactive waste) facility under the Baltic Sea, operational since 1988, received approval in 2024 to triple its capacity. • France: ANDRA applied in 2023 to build the Cigéo geological disposal site in northeastern France. • Canada: In 2024, the Nuclear Waste Management Organization selected Wabigoon Lake Ojibway Nation and Ignace as host communities for its deep repository. • Switzerland: In 2024, Nagra applied for a federal permit to build a repository and spent fuel encapsulation plant with a final federal decision awaited in 2029-30. 0 2,000 4,000 6,000 8,000 10,000 12,000 14,000 16,000 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 Orano Rosatom Cameco ConverDyn Unspecified 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 0 5,000 10,000 15,000 20,000 25,000 30,000 Total Net Electrical Capacity Number of Reactors

 The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 33 The emerging global nuclear decommissioning industry As many nuclear power plants (NPPs) approach the end of their operational life, a vast new global industry dedicated to nuclear decommissioning is taking shape. Beyond power reactors, numerous other facilities involved in the nuclear fuel cycle, such as research centres, reprocessing plants, and waste treatment sites, will also need to be dismantled safely. Between now and 2050, hundreds of billions of dollars are widely expected to be spent on decommissioning activities worldwide, drawing strong interest from engineering firms, service providers, and investors positioning themselves in this growing market. A decommissioning plan is required before construction of nuclear plants Currently, around 420 reactors operate globally, while roughly 200 are due to enter a decommissioning phase by 2050. Given that dismantling a single plant can take two decades or more, the industry’s workload is substantial and long-term. Moreover, every new nuclear project now requires a decommissioning plan before construction begins, further strengthening the future pipeline for decommissioning expertise. The sector relies heavily on engineering, construction, waste management, and demolition companies, whose role is to safely dismantle facilities, decontaminate materials, and restore sites for future use. Environmental and sustainability principles are becoming central to this process: recovered metals, cables, and clean concrete are recycled wherever possible, and waste is carefully segregated to minimise the environmental impact. To manage this complex, highly regulated work, the industry also requires a specialised and expanding workforce to avoid future skill shortages. To promote safety and efficiency, the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) plays a pivotal role through its International Decommissioning Network, which connects organisations and experts to share knowledge, best practices, and lessons learned. The IAEA also provides technical, legal, and safety guidance, conducts reviews, and supports training and workshops worldwide. A leading example of decommissioning expertise comes from Sellafield in the United Kingdom, one of the world’s most complex nuclear sites. Built in the 1950s and home to Calder Hall, the world’s first commercial nuclear power station, the site includes numerous legacy facilities in proximity. Decommissioning began in the 1980s and is set to continue well into the next century. Sellafield has developed innovative digital and robotic solutions for dismantling and waste management and shares this expertise internationally to help improve design and operational standards for future facilities. The economic impact of decommissioning is significant. In 2021, the UK’s Nuclear Decommissioning Authority, which oversees Sellafield, spent about 55% of its USD 4bn annual budget on services provided by partner companies, illustrating the substantial supply-chain opportunities associated with this field. Globally, experience from past projects is helping to accelerate decommissioning timelines and reduce costs. In the US, recent projects aim to complete the main dismantling phase of commercial reactors in five to seven years, roughly half the historical average. This is achieved through improved project coordination, early regulatory approvals, and efficient waste recycling. Early authorisation for decommissioning, as seen in Germany, also shortens the overall project duration by enabling work to start immediately after plant shutdown. Furthermore, increasing the recycling and re-use of materials from dismantled facilities plays a key role in cutting costs and advancing circular economic principles in the nuclear sector. Decommissioning and waste management set to expand operations Overall, the global decommissioning industry is entering a period of rapid growth and professionalisation. It combines high technical complexity with significant environmental and economic opportunities, transforming nuclear end-of-life management into one of the most dynamic and strategically important segments of the global energy transition. Conclusion The global nuclear sector is entering a decisive end-of-life phase that will shape capacity, costs, and policy for decades. Even with the growing trend toward lifetime extensions to 60 years, and in some cases 80, the age profile of the fleet implies a large, unavoidable retirement wave. Under a conservative 40-year assumption, nearly 100 additional reactors would be expected to shut by 2030, with more than 150 further closures likely by 2065, on top of units already beyond 40 years and those in long-term outage. While life-extension programmes will soften the cliff, they will not eliminate it. Recent additions/retirements in capacity (MW) Source: BNEF (January 2026) Decommissioning is emerging as a strategic bottleneck. More than 200 reactors have been permanently closed worldwide, representing over 100GW of retired capacity, yet only a small subset has completed the full decommissioning cycle and even fewer sites have been returned to greenfield status. Most shut units are partway through lengthy, complex dismantling processes that frequently last two decades or more, often longer than the plants operated.
