The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 41 European nuclear capacity expansion has evolved in phases Over the past six decades, nuclear power development in the EU27 has unfolded in distinct waves. After two relatively modest construction phases in the 1960s and 1970s, the largest expansion occurred in the 1980s, driven mainly by France. Since then, however, nuclear newbuild has slowed dramatically. Between 1995 and 2024, only 13 reactors were connected to the grid across today’s EU27. Six of these were in Western Europe, five in France and one in Finland, while seven were completed in Central and Eastern Europe, notably in Slovakia, the Czech Republic, and Romania. The pace has been especially slow in the past two decades: from 2005 to 2024, only four reactors entered service. After Romania’s Cernavodă-2 in 2007, Europe waited 15 years for the next startup, Finland’s Olkiluoto-3 in 2022, followed by Mochovce-3 in Slovakia in 2023 and Flamanville-3 in France at the end of 2024. EU27 reactor starts & retirements (no of reactors) Source: WNISR (mid-2025) Over the last 20 years, 36 nuclear plants have been closed in Europe As of mid-2025, 100 nuclear reactors were operating in the EU27, a sharp decline from the historical peak of 136 units in 1989. This represents a reduction of more than one quarter over three and a half decades. Nuclear capacity is also unevenly distributed. Around 80% of operating reactors are concentrated in six Western European countries, with France alone accounting for 57 units, while only 20 reactors are spread across six newer member states. Reactor closures have continued in recent years, most notably in Belgium and Germany, bringing the total number of permanently shut down reactors in the EU27 to 78. More than half of these closures occurred in Western Europe, particularly in Germany. Over the last 20 years alone, 36 reactors have been retired. Electricity production from nuclear power has fluctuated significantly. In 2024, EU27 nuclear plants generated about 617TWh net, nearly 5% more than in 2023, following a modest recovery that came after a steep 17% drop in 2022. As a result, nuclear’s share of electricity generation fell to just under 22% in 2022, before edging back up to around 23% by 2024. These changes reflect both operational challenges and the long-term contraction of the fleet. Average age of existing European nuclear reactors is 39 years With very limited new construction, the European nuclear fleet continues to age. The average reactor age reached nearly 39 years by mid-2025. Almost nine out of 10 operating reactors have been in service for more than 30 years, and 42 have exceeded 40 years of operation. The Netherlands’ Borssele reactor became the oldest in the EU, passing 51 years, in 2024, while Belgium’s Doel-1 was closed shortly after its 50th anniversary in early 2025. Following the recent startups in Finland, Slovakia, and France, only one reactor remains under construction within the EU27: Mochovce-4 in Slovakia, a project whose origins date back to 1985. Once this unit finally enters service, likely in late 2025 or early 2026, no further reactor startups are scheduled for several years, meaning the fleet will continue to age and gradually shrink in the medium term. European nuclear market needs to address challenges In June 2025, the European Commission published its long-awaited Nuclear Illustrative Programme. It estimates that around EUR 241bn will be needed by 2050 to support lifetime extensions and limited newbuild, excluding small modular and advanced reactors. Even with these investments, the EC projects total installed nuclear capacity to rise only marginally, from about 98GW in 2025 to roughly 109GW by 2050 and could be much lower if projects face significant delays. The Commission openly acknowledges that Europe’s nuclear industry faces major challenges, including limited industrial capacity, skills shortages, and an image problem that makes the sector unattractive to younger generations. Overall, while nuclear power remains an important part of Europe’s electricity system, the absence of a robust newbuild pipeline, combined with an aging reactor fleet and ongoing closures, points to a future in which nuclear capacity is likely to stagnate or decline rather than expand significantly. Belgium Belgium’s nuclear fleet has steadily contracted over the past two decades because of a phaseout policy adopted in 2003, which capped reactor lifetimes at 40 years and banned new construction. From an original seven reactors, only four units, two at Doel and two at Tihange, with a combined capacity of about 3.5GW, remained connected to the grid as of 1 July 2025. The most recent closure was Doel-1, a 445MW reactor that was shut down in February 2025 in line with the phaseout law. In 2021, the government confirmed that all remaining reactors would close by 2025, but this trajectory changed after the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 exposed energy security risks and triggered a reassessment of nuclear power’s role. -10 -5 0 5 10 15

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 42 Belgium extends lifetime of operational reactors As a result, the government-initiated steps to extend the operating lives of the two youngest reactors, Doel-4 and Tihange-3, which were then around 40 years old. Negotiations with Electrabel, the Engie-owned operator, led to an interim agreement in 2023 to extend both units by 10 years. Under this deal, the Belgian state becomes an equal co-owner alongside Engie and introduces a contract-for-difference mechanism designed to balance risk and performance incentives. A central element of the agreement is a EUR 15bn lump-sum payment from Engie to the state, covering all future nuclear waste management costs for the entire Belgian nuclear fleet, including already closed reactors. The state and Electrabel also committed to financing the bulk of the EUR 2-2.5bn needed for safety upgrades and refurbishment, with the remainder coming from Luminus, a subsidiary of EDF that holds minority shares in the plants. While the legal framework enabling the lifetime extensions was adopted in April 2024, some details of the financing structure were still unresolved as of early 2025. The European Commission approved the arrangement in February 2025 after assessing its compatibility with EU state aid rules, and the final agreement was signed in March 2025. This cleared the way for practical preparations, including fuel procurement, regulatory approvals from Belgium’s nuclear safety authority, and major engineering work during planned outages in 2025. A first tranche of the waste-related payment was transferred to a newly created public agency, with the remainder due once both reactors returned to service in late 2025. Under current law, Doel-4 and Tihange-3 may operate for up to 10 additional years, but no later than the end of 2037. Despite these extensions, nuclear output is set to decline in the short term as older reactors close. Nuclear energy still plays a dominant role in Belgium’s electricity mix, generating around 42 % of electricity in 2024, although this is well below the historic peak of more than two-thirds in the mid-1980s. Natural gas, wind, solar, bioenergy, and hydro make up the remainder. With Doel-1 already closed and Tihange-1 and Doel-2 due to shut down later in 2025, total nuclear generation will fall further before stabilising around the two extended units. At the policy level, Belgium’s energy strategy has become increasingly unsettled. Long-term plans continue to emphasise carbon neutrality by 2050, largely through a major expansion of renewable energy, particularly offshore wind. However, the country missed deadlines to submit an updated National Energy and Climate Plan to the European Commission, which launched infringement proceedings in 2024. Following federal elections in June 2024, a new five-party “Arizona coalition” took office in February 2025 under prime minister De Wever. This government has signalled a sharp turn back towards nuclear power, promising major changes to the energy system, securing around 4GW of existing nuclear capacity in the short term, lifting the legal ban on new reactors, and seeking to accelerate nuclear construction and licensing, including for small modular reactors. This political shift culminated in May 2025 with the repeal of the phaseout law and the removal of the ban on new nuclear plants. Yet significant uncertainties remain. Engie, the main operator, has publicly stated that further lifetime extensions beyond those already agreed are unrealistic and that nuclear energy no longer fits its long-term strategy. Other players, notably Luminus and EDF-linked entities, have expressed greater openness to cooperation, while also emphasising investments in renewables, storage, and flexibility. In parallel, Belgium has seen substantial growth in renewable capacity over the past decade, especially in solar power and both onshore and offshore wind. Solar installations have more than tripled since 2015, and offshore wind capacity has expanded rapidly, although growth has slowed in recent years. New offshore projects in the Princess Elisabeth zone are now moving forward, with plans to reach nearly 6GW of offshore wind by 2030. Against this backdrop, Belgium’s energy future remains contested, with nuclear power regaining political momentum even as renewables continue to expand and key industrial actors remain cautious about a full-scale nuclear revival. Finland Nuclear power remained a central pillar of Finland’s electricity system in 2024, even though output declined. Nuclear reactors generated 31.3TWh of electricity during 2025, corresponding to approximately 40% of Finnish power generation. Finland’s reactor fleet reflects a mix of technologies and suppliers: two Russian-built VVER units at Loviisa, two Swedish-designed boiling water reactors at Olkiluoto dating from the late 1970s, and the much newer Olkiluoto-3 reactor, a 1,575MW European Pressurised Reactor that represents a third-generation design. The most recent addition to the Finnish nuclear fleet proved a challenging project, with significant delays Olkiluoto-3 has had a particularly complex history. Built by the Areva–Siemens consortium, the project entered commercial operation more than a decade behind schedule and only reached full output for the first time in April 2023, nearly 18 years after construction began. Although joint liabilities between the operator TVO and the vendors were finally resolved in June 2025, the plant’s early operational years have been marked by technical challenges. In 2024, its first annual maintenance outage took far longer than planned, and the reactor suffered a series of unexpected events later in the year, including a dropped control rod and a turbine malfunction that caused a major frequency disturbance across the Nordic power system. These issues contributed to lower-than- expected nuclear generation and raised concerns about reliability, particularly as the unit also had to reduce output at times because of high wind generation and grid constraints. Overall electricity production in Finland in 2024 covered about 96% of domestic demand, falling just short of full self- sufficiency. Unplanned nuclear outages played a role, even though they coincided with a record year for wind power, which produced more than 20TWh. The situation was further complicated by reduced interconnection capacity after

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 43 damage to the EstLink 2 cable between Finland and Estonia, limiting the system’s flexibility until the line returned to service in mid-2025. Despite these challenges, the high share of domestic generation helped stabilise the grid during a period of constrained cross-border capacity. Operational difficulties were not limited to Olkiluoto-3. Olkiluoto-2 was taken offline in September 2024 after excessive moisture was found in its generator, leading to a lengthy repair and months of operation at reduced capacity. When the unit was being ramped back up in April 2025, the problem re-occurred, forcing another shutdown and replacement of a refurbished spare rotor. As a result, Olkiluoto-2 is expected by TVO, the operator, to continue operating below full power until its next major outage in 2026. Olkiluoto-1 also experienced a brief disruption in May 2025 due to a turbine-side malfunction, while both reactors at the Loviisa plant underwent routine maintenance and there were several shorter unplanned events, including a cooling system issue and a hydrogen leak that temporarily reduced output. Alongside these operational challenges, Finland is actively planning for long-term nuclear operation. By mid-2025, the four reactors already subject to lifetime extensions had an average age of more than 46 years. Olkiluoto-1 and -2 have licences allowing operation until 2038, but operator TVO is now exploring further extensions to 2048 or even 2058, combined with an 8% power uprate. An environmental impact assessment submitted in late 2024 was approved by the Ministry of Economic Affairs and Employment in April 2025, enabling TVO to move forward with substantial investments aimed primarily at safety upgrades, such as modernising instrumentation and control systems. Although framed as safety-focused, these investments explicitly consider the possibility of longer operating lifetimes and higher output. At Loviisa, the long-term commitment to nuclear power is even clearer. Both reactors were granted lifetime extensions in 2023 allowing operation until 2050, which would result in planned operating lives of around 70 years. Modernisation work scheduled from 2026 onwards is expected by the operator to slightly increase capacity, and the operator estimates that total investments of about EUR 1bn will be required by mid-century to sustain safe operation. Taken together, these developments show that while Finland’s nuclear fleet has faced notable reliability issues in recent years, particularly at its newest reactor, the country remains firmly committed to nuclear power as a long-term component of its low-carbon electricity system. Finland has taken concrete steps to diversify its nuclear fuel supply by reducing its reliance on Russia. In August 2024, Fortum loaded the first Westinghouse-manufactured fuel assemblies into the Loviisa-2 reactor, marking a significant shift away from the Russian supplier TVEL. Additional fuel deliveries are planned from 2025 onwards through Enusa of Spain, following its long-term cooperation agreement with Westinghouse. At the same time, Finland continues to navigate the fallout from the cancelled Hanhikivi nuclear project, including legal disputes triggered by Rosatom’s multibillion- dollar lawsuit. Despite these challenges, parts of the Finnish energy sector and government remain open to new nuclear projects, provided future developers meet strict national security requirements. French nuclear capacity recovering output fall as service backlog France France’s nuclear sector experienced a partial rebound in 2024, with electricity generation rising by more than 40TWh from 2023 to 361.7TWh, equivalent to about 67% of national power production, and then to 367TWh in 2025. While this marked a significant improvement after the deep slump of 2022, output remained well below the levels that once defined the French nuclear model. Between 2005 and 2015, annual nuclear production typically hovered around 400TWh and peaked at 430TWh in 2005. For the ninth consecutive year, generation has stayed below that threshold, and nuclear output has declined across most of the rest of the European Union. France aims to generate over 400TWh/year of nuclear power in the future although the exact timing is uncertain. French nuclear output and share (TWh/%) Source: WNISR , IAEA (January 2026) Flamanville 3 has been completed after more than a decade of delays A symbolic milestone was reached in December 2024 with the long-awaited grid connection of Flamanville-3, France’s first European Pressurised Reactor. This occurred 17 years after construction began, 12 years later than originally planned, and 25 years after the previous new reactor, Civaux-2, entered service. The start-up has been troubled. France’s newly created Nuclear Safety and Radiation Protection Authority reported an unusually high number of significant safety events during commissioning, highlighting persistent operational and organisational weaknesses rather than a smooth transition to routine operation. France harbours ambitions to expand new nuclear capacity Despite these difficulties, the French government has doubled down on nuclear power. Legislation adopted in 2023 was designed to accelerate procedures for new reactors near existing sites and to facilitate extended operation of the current fleet. The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 44 plans for six new EPR2 reactors, with the possibility of eight more by mid-century. Yet the EPR2 programme remains at an early and uncertain stage. Detailed designs are still incomplete, cost estimates remain provisional, and financing arrangements have not been finalised. Although EDF has already ordered major components and begun site preparations, it does not expect a final investment decision before late 2026 at the earliest. France’s EPR2 programme would signal a renewed push to expand the country’s nuclear capacity and rebuild its industrial base. It involves constructing six next-generation EPR2 reactors, three identical pairs, at the Penly, Gravelines, and Bugey sites. Each unit is intended to deliver around 1,670 MW, and the shift to a fully standardised design reflects lessons learned from earlier EPR projects, including Flamanville 3, Taishan, and Hinkley Point C. The EPR2 model is deliberately more modular and “industrialised,” with a simplified layout and greater use of repeatable construction methods intended to shorten build times, lower complexity, and improve reliability. The overall programme is estimated by the French government to cost EUR 72.8bn in 2020 value, underlining both its scale and the degree of political commitment behind it. EDF has already approved funding allocations for 2026, and state-supported loans are set to play a significant role in managing the financial burden and keeping the utility’s balance sheet resilient. Although initial political rhetoric suggested earlier commissioning, the schedule has since been pushed back: Construction of the first pair at Penly is now due to begin in 2027-28, with the first reactors not entering service until 2038-40. This reflects both the engineering challenge and the need to rebuild industrial and supply-chain capacity after decades with little new nuclear construction in France. Nuclear industrial capacity expansion is already underway Despite the long lead times, industrial mobilisation is already underway. Framatome and other suppliers have started producing key components, indicating that preparatory work is progressing and that long-lead manufacturing is being aligned with the revised timeline. Overall, the EPR2 programme signals France’s attempt to re-establish itself as a leader in nuclear construction through a combination of political resolve, industrial reorganisation, and a more pragmatic, standardised approach to reactor design. Institutional changes have also reshaped nuclear governance. At the start of 2025, the former separation between the Nuclear Safety Authority and its independent technical support body was ended through their merger into a single authority. This move, strongly contested across the political spectrum and by trade unions, reversed decades of efforts to reinforce institutional independence in nuclear oversight. At the same time, strategic decisions are increasingly centralised within the Nuclear Policy Council, chaired by the President, rather than being debated in Parliament. Aging fleet in need of service and restoration Operationally, the existing fleet of 57 pressurised water reactors showed improvement in 2024, with fewer reactors simultaneously offline and shorter cumulative outage durations than in the crisis years of 2022 and 2023. Nevertheless, unavailability remained high by historical standards, averaging about three months per reactor. Forced outages, often linked to technical issues, stayed at elevated levels. Over the five-year period from 2020 to 2024, most reactors were offline for at least a quarter of the time, underscoring the fragility of an aging fleet. A major source of uncertainty continues to be stress corrosion cracking and thermal fatigue in safety-related piping. First identified in 2021, these phenomena have required extensive inspections, repairs, and replacements across much of the fleet. Although EDF maintains that detection and remediation techniques are now under control, new cracks were still being discovered in 2025, and safety authorities have called for additional investigations and more cautious assumptions. EDF itself expects some planned outages to be extended due to these issues. Lifetime extension where possible to support underlying generation capacity Lifetime extensions are therefore both essential and demanding. The average age of French reactors now exceeds 39 years, with most units already past the 40-year mark. Fourth decennial safety reviews, which are prerequisites for continued operation, involve work volumes far greater than previous inspections and have frequently exceeded planned durations by months. While the regulator has shown flexibility in allowing phased implementation of safety upgrades, this has stretched refurbishment programmes over decades and placed heavy strain on industrial capacity, skills, and finances. EDF’s debt ballooned during years of low output and extended outages, then stabilised as production recovered in 2023 and 2024. However, the company remains highly exposed to electricity price volatility. The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 45 eroding revenues and forcing greater modulation of nuclear output. Meanwhile, the Flamanville-3 project stands as a cautionary tale, with total costs including financing and new grid connections now estimated at more than EUR 23bn and projected electricity costs by the French Court of Accounts, well above current market prices. The Flamanville 3 reactor’s cost has surged from an initial EUR 3.3bn to between EUR 13.2bn and EUR 23.7bn as of early 2025, with the higher figure reflecting full financing costs. Years of delays, technical setbacks such as faulty weld repairs, and regulatory demands have significantly contributed to the overruns. France’s draft energy plan for 2030-35 reflects this tension. It assumes stable nuclear production around today’s levels, no reactor closures, and continued lifetime extensions, while also projecting strong growth in solar and wind power. In practice, France is navigating a narrow path: relying on an aging nuclear fleet with unresolved technical challenges, pursuing an expensive and risky newbuild programme, and simultaneously integrating rapidly expanding renewables. The improvement seen in 2024 may have eased immediate concerns, but it has not resolved the deeper structural, financial, and industrial challenges facing the French nuclear model. Dutch nuclear capacity consists of one nuclear plant The Netherlands The Netherlands today operates only one nuclear power reactor, the 482MW pressurised water reactor at Borssele, which is more than 50 years old and the oldest operating nuclear plant in the European Union. In 2024, Borssele generated about 3.4TWh of electricity, roughly 10% less than the year before, and accounted for a stable but modest 2.9% of national electricity production. This is far below the historic peak of 6.2% reached in the mid-1980s, when the country still operated a second, much smaller reactor at Dodewaard. That reactor was shut down in 1997 and is currently in a long-term safe enclosure phase, with dismantling scheduled decades after fuel removal was completed. Despite its age, Borssele remains central to the Netherlands’ nuclear debate. Plans to extend its operating lifetime beyond the current 2033 closure date, potentially by up to 20 years, are progressing but remain uncertain. Such an extension would allow the reactor to operate for as long as 80 years, a highly unusual lifespan by international standards. The government’s draft Climate Fund Plan for 2026 confirms that it is investigating whether a safe and efficient lifetime extension is feasible. A pre-SALTO mission by the International Atomic Energy Agency in late 2024 identified a series of technical and organisational issues that would need to be resolved, particularly around aging management for mechanical, electrical, and civil structures, as well as the long-term availability of skilled personnel. Borssele is operated by EPZ, which is majority-owned by a public holding company representing the province of Zeeland and local municipalities, with German utility RWE holding the remaining shares. Any lifetime extension would require amendments to the existing Borssele covenant, changes to the Nuclear Energy Act, and approval from regulators and shareholders alike. EPZ has stated that it needs clarity by 2029 on whether the plant will continue operating or move toward decommissioning. While the government had hoped to complete the necessary legal changes by 2026 so that a licence application could follow in 2027, political instability has complicated the timeline. The collapse of the Schoof government in mid-2025 and the announcement of snap elections have added uncertainty, as has the fact that shareholders have indicated they would only support a lifetime extension if the state assumed most of the financial risk. At the same time, Dutch nuclear policy has shifted markedly toward new construction. In recent years, successive governments have moved from cautious consideration to explicit support for nuclear expansion, even as advisory bodies warned of potential grid overcapacity. Following the 2023 elections, the new right-wing coalition committed to building four new reactors and earmarked EUR 14bn for nuclear power through 2035. However, officials have since acknowledged that this amount is likely insufficient. By early 2025, the energy minister openly stated that even two new reactors would probably require EUR 20-30bn and that the state would need to finance the early construction phases to attract private investors. As a result, earlier ambitions to have a new plant operating by 2035 can no longer be considered realistic. Site selection and technical feasibility remain contentious. The Borssele site is often mentioned as a candidate, but expanding nuclear capacity there would require major grid reinforcements due to the already high concentration of nearby wind power. Another proposed site in Groningen faces strong local and provincial opposition. A safety review commissioned by the nuclear regulator concluded in 2025 that reactor designs from Westinghouse, EDF, and South Korea’s KHNP could, in principle, be built safely in the Netherlands, though this did not guarantee licensing approval. Shortly afterward, KHNP withdrew from consideration, leaving Westinghouse and EDF as the remaining potential vendors. Alongside large reactors, interest in small modular reactors has emerged at the regional level. Studies commissioned by provincial authorities and research institutions have identified several potentially suitable locations and suggested that SMRs could play a role in supplying both electricity and industrial heat. Nevertheless, these initiatives remain exploratory, with no binding commitments, and the national government’s primary focus continues to be on large-scale reactors rather than SMRs. Meanwhile, the Dutch electricity system is changing rapidly regardless of nuclear policy. In 2024, renewable sources generated just over half of total electricity, surpassing fossil fuels and nuclear combined. Wind and solar power have expanded at remarkable speed, with significant additions in both offshore wind and rooftop photovoltaics, making the Netherlands a global leader in solar capacity per capita. Natural gas still dominates fossil generation, but its role is shrinking. While nuclear power remains politically prominent, its actual contribution to the electricity mix is small, and environmental authorities have warned that additional policy

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 46 measures will be needed to meet near-term climate targets, regardless of whether new nuclear projects ultimately materialise. Spain As of mid-2025, Spain operates seven nuclear reactors with a combined capacity of about 7.1GW. In 2024, these plants produced 51.9TWh of electricity, supplying just over 19% of national electricity demand. This is a far cry from nuclear power’s peak contribution of more than 38% in 1989 and reflects both the aging nature of the fleet and the rapid growth of renewable generation. The average operating age of Spanish reactors now exceeds 40 years, placing Spain among the European countries with the oldest nuclear fleets. Ownership of Spain’s nuclear plants is concentrated among a small group of utilities, primarily Iberdrola and Endesa, often in partnership with Naturgy and, in one case, EDP. This concentration has shaped negotiations between operators and the state, particularly around Spain’s long-standing nuclear phaseout policy. In 2019, the government reached an agreement with the main utilities as part of the country’s Integrated National Energy and Climate Plan, setting a timetable for the gradual closure of all reactors by 2035. At the same time, the agreement allowed operators to apply for lifetime extensions beyond 40 years, a more flexible approach than earlier Socialist Party proposals, which had envisaged only short deferrals of closures originally planned for the mid-2020s. The phaseout schedule was reaffirmed in Spain’s updated National Energy and Climate Plan published in 2024. Spain nuclear fleet outlook Reactor Net capacity (MW) Grid connection Licence expiration Expected closure Almaraz-1 1,011 1981 2027 2027 Almaraz- 2 1,006 1983 2028 2028 Asco-1 995 1983 2030 2030 Cofrentes 1,064 1984 2030 2030 Asco-2 997 1985 2031 2032 Vandellos-2 1,047 1987 2030 2035 Trillo-1 1,003 1988 2034 2035 Source: WNISR, IAEA Over the past five years, all Spanish reactors have received operating licence extensions beyond their initial 40-year design lives, but the closure timetable still begins later this decade. Almaraz-1 and Almaraz-2 are currently scheduled to shut down in 2027 and 2028, respectively, followed by Ascó-1 and Cofrentes around 2030, with the final reactors closing by 2035. For several units, existing licences will expire before their planned closure dates, meaning further extensions will be required if the agreed schedule is to be maintained. The Spanish renewables market has grown rapidly The economic environment for nuclear power in Spain has become increasingly challenging. The electricity market has seen a growing number of hours with very low or even negative prices, driven by high output from wind, solar, and hydroelectric plants. During periods of strong renewable generation, nuclear reactors have been partially or fully disconnected from the grid because operating at market prices was no longer profitable. Such situations occurred during the Easter period in 2024 and again in spring 2025, when wholesale prices fell to exceptionally low levels. Nuclear operators and their industry association argue that a heavy tax burden, particularly fees for radioactive waste management and decommissioning, exacerbates these pressures. In mid-2024, the levy financing nuclear waste management was significantly increased following an upward revision of long-term waste cost estimates. Utilities have challenged both the revised waste plan and the higher fees in court, claiming they violate the 2019 phaseout agreement and seeking substantial compensation. Against this backdrop, political opposition to the nuclear phaseout has intensified. Although the conservative Popular Party won the most seats in the 2023 elections, it failed to form a government, and Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez’s Socialist-led coalition remained in power into 2025 amid growing political strain. The Popular Party campaigned on reversing the nuclear phaseout and, in February 2025, succeeded in passing a non-binding parliamentary motion calling for lifetime extensions and improved economic conditions for nuclear plants. The vote exposed deep political divisions, with right-wing parties in favour and left-wing and regional parties largely opposed or abstaining. Nuclear power plays a stabilising role Following the vote, industry pressure mounted. Nuclear companies and suppliers, supported by firms from other sectors, issued public calls to renegotiate the 2019 agreement and revise the national energy plan to ensure continued nuclear operation. Regional leaders joined the debate, particularly in areas hosting nuclear plants. The president of Extremadura, home to the Almaraz plant, argued for extending its operation and even suggested state takeover if taxes were not reduced, while leaders in Madrid described nuclear power as essential for grid stability and economic development. Senior executives at Iberdrola warned that closing nuclear plants could raise electricity prices significantly and undermine system reliability, framing the issue as a pragmatic energy security choice rather than an ideological one. The debate intensified further after the massive blackout that struck Spain and Portugal on 28 April 2025. The outage, the most severe in recent Iberian history, was quickly linked in public discourse to the high share of renewables in the electricity mix at the time, even before investigations were completed. A government inquiry later concluded that the blackout resulted from a complex chain of events, including miscalculations by the grid operator, inadequate responses from plants tasked with stabilising voltage, and rapid disconnections that overwhelmed the system. Renewables themselves were not identified as the root cause, though the incident highlighted vulnerabilities in grid management and coordination. In response, the government adopted urgent measures to strengthen system oversight, expand storage and flexibility, and accelerate electrification, while long-planned

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 47 investments in interconnections with France and energy storage moved forward. Despite the controversy, Spain remains firmly committed to its long-term climate goals. The government aims for climate neutrality by 2050 and for renewables to supply more than 80% of electricity by 2030. In 2024 alone, Spain added 7.3GW of new renewable capacity, mostly solar photovoltaic, bringing total renewable capacity to over 85GW. As a result, the electricity mix is now dominated by wind and solar, with nuclear providing under 20% and fossil fuels continuing to decline. In this rapidly evolving system, nuclear power occupies an increasingly contested role, balancing aging infrastructure, economic pressures, political division, and the accelerating momentum of renewable energy. Nuclear power is central to the Swedish energy mix Sweden Sweden’s nuclear power output rose modestly in 2024, with the country’s six remaining reactors producing 48.1TWh of electricity, about 4.4% more than the year before. Nuclear energy accounted for just under 30% of total electricity generation, a sharp contrast to the mid-1990s, when nuclear supplied more than half of Sweden’s power and the fleet still comprised 12 reactors. Although nuclear remains an important component of the system, Sweden’s electricity mix has changed fundamentally over the past two decades, with renewables now dominating overall production. Aging fleet to receive lifetime extension along with potential new capacity The Swedish nuclear fleet is aging, with an average reactor age of around 43 years by mid-2025. Four of the six reactors are already more than 40 years old, prompting operators to explore further lifetime extensions. In 2024, Vattenfall, which operates the reactors at Forsmark and Ringhals, announced a strategic intention to assess extending plant lifetimes from 60 to as much as 80 years, potentially keeping them on the grid into the 2060s. Similar plans emerged for Oskarshamn-3, whose owners, Uniper and Fortum, are studying options to continue operating the plant well beyond its current closure date in 2045. These initiatives remain under investigation, with final investment decisions expected by the operators only later in the decade and subject to stringent regulatory reviews. Operational reliability has been a persistent challenge for Sweden’s nuclear fleet in recent years. While conditions at Ringhals stabilised after severe difficulties in 2022, Forsmark-3 experienced an extended outage starting in autumn 2024 due to cracks in turbine components, delaying its return to full service until early 2025. Even after restarting, the reactor suffered an additional short outage caused by a faulty valve. At Oskarshamn-3, a planned maintenance stops in spring 2025 was repeatedly extended when cracks were discovered in critical piping, pushing the restart from early summer to late summer and eventually to September. These incidents underscored the technical risks associated with operating aging nuclear infrastructure, even as operators pursue extended lifetimes. A reversal of long-held expectations relating to decommissioning of nuclear power Alongside efforts to prolong existing reactors, Sweden has undergone a major policy reversal toward new nuclear construction. In 2023, the government replaced its long-standing target of “100% renewable” electricity by 2040 with a goal of “100% fossil-free” power, explicitly reopening the door to nuclear newbuild. Official plans now envision adding around 2.5GW of new nuclear capacity by 2035 and expanding to the equivalent of 10 large reactors by 2045. To coordinate this effort, the government appointed a national nuclear new-build coordinator tasked with identifying regulatory, financial, and organisational barriers and proposing solutions. Interim reports stress that licensing timelines, estimated at up to 10 years from application to grid connection, represent the most significant bottleneck, making parallel construction projects at multiple sites essential for meeting government targets. The policy shift has been backed by new funding and legislative measures. In its 2025 budget, the government allocated dedicated resources to support fossil-free electricity generation, including funding for nuclear pilot and demonstration projects and preparatory work on permitting frameworks. Recognising that private investors are reluctant to bear the financial risks of nuclear construction, Sweden introduced a new financing model in 2025 that allows the state to cover up to 75% of project investment costs through government loans. For a hypothetical four-reactor programme, this could translate into state backing of more than SEK 300bn. The legislation also includes a contracts-for-difference mechanism that guarantees fixed revenues to operators, funded by a small levy on electricity consumers. Even so, this state aid framework still requires approval from the European Commission, and the absence of concrete projects means many details remain unresolved. Big plans but no formal decision yet Despite confident political rhetoric, including assertions that construction of a new nuclear plant could begin before the 2026 election, scepticism is widespread. No Swedish utility has yet committed to building a new reactor or chosen a final technology supplier. Vattenfall focuses on SMR-technology while Fortum, which holds stakes in existing Swedish reactors, has stated that while it remains interested in nuclear as a long-term option, new demand in the Nordic electricity market over the next decade will likely be met more quickly and cheaply by wind, solar, storage, and lifetime extensions of existing plants rather than by new nuclear builds. Critics argue that Sweden’s nuclear focus risks distracting from a highly successful renewable energy transition. Environmental groups warn that delays or failures in nuclear construction could undermine climate targets if renewable expansion falters. Indeed, permitting obstacles have slowed onshore wind development, and security concerns led to the cancellation of numerous offshore wind projects in 2024. At the same time, subsidies for solar power were reduced even as

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 48 photovoltaic capacity continued to grow rapidly, surpassing an installed capacity of 5GW by 2024. In practice, Sweden’s electricity system is already overwhelmingly renewable. In 2024, nearly 70% of electricity generation came from renewable sources, led by hydropower and wind, with nuclear contributing just under 30% and fossil fuels playing only a marginal role. This reality highlights the central tension in Sweden’s energy debate: whether expanding nuclear power is a necessary complement to a fossil-free system or a costly and uncertain diversion from a transition that has already delivered impressive results through renewables. Switzerland Switzerland’s nuclear power output declined slightly in 2024, with gross production falling by about 1.5% y/y to 23TWh. Because overall electricity generation reached a record 81TWh, driven largely by exceptionally strong hydropower output, nuclear power’s share of the mix dropped to 28.4%. This is well below the historical peak of 43% recorded in the mid-1990s and reflects the long-term shift in Switzerland’s electricity system toward renewables, particularly hydro and, increasingly, solar power. An aging Swiss nuclear fleet drives the need to review policy decision Switzerland operates one of the oldest nuclear fleets in the world. The average age of its four operating reactors was over 49 as of mid-2025, making the fleet second only to the Netherlands in age. Beznau-1, at 56 years, is the oldest commercial nuclear reactor still in operation globally, with Beznau-2 close behind at nearly 54 years. The Mühleberg reactor was shut down in 2019 and is currently undergoing decommissioning, leaving Beznau, Gösgen, and Leibstadt as the remaining nuclear sites. Unlike many countries, Switzerland grants nuclear operating licences for an unlimited period, meaning reactors may continue operating if they meet safety requirements set by the regulator. In practice, operators of all remaining plants have sought to extend operation to at least 60 years, despite ongoing public and expert debates over safety issues, particularly at Beznau-1 and Leibstadt. Concerns have focused on material degradation and other age-related vulnerabilities that have been discussed extensively in past assessments. In March 2024, Axpo launched a formal study on the technical feasibility of operating the Beznau plant beyond 60 years. Later that year, the company announced a firm decision to shut down Beznau-1 in 2033 and Beznau-2 in 2032, extending their operating lives to 64 and 61 years, respectively. To support this extended operation, Axpo committed to invest an additional CHF 350m in safety upgrades, on top of the CHF 2.5bn already spent over the plant’s lifetime. The Swiss nuclear safety regulator acknowledged the operator’s plans and confirmed that the next periodic safety review is scheduled for 2027, although procedural timelines mean documentation will likely need to be submitted earlier. Even with this planned extension, there are limits to how long Beznau can operate. Experts have pointed to flaws in the reactor pressure vessel steel of Beznau-1, first identified in 2015, as a key constraint on further lifetime extensions. In addition, the plant’s reliability has come under scrutiny. Unplanned outages have become more frequent, and Beznau-1’s load factor dropped sharply in 2024. Technical issues unrelated to the nuclear core, such as valve failures, cable repairs, and follow-up checks on steel components, nonetheless led to prolonged shutdowns. During hot summer periods in 2024 and 2025, both Beznau units also had to reduce output or shut down temporarily to protect the Aare River from excessive thermal stress. Possible lifetime extension and a review of new capacity At the other Swiss nuclear plants, lifetime extension discussions remain less advanced. As of mid-2024, the operators of Gösgen and Leibstadt had not carried out in-depth studies on operating beyond 60 years, citing political uncertainty as a major obstacle. Current plans still foresee closures in 2039 for Gösgen and 2044 for Leibstadt, corresponding to 60 years of operation, although some political voices have already begun advocating for 80-year lifetimes. Beyond plant lifetimes, Switzerland is also grappling with renewed political debates over nuclear newbuild. The Energy Strategy 2050, approved by referendum in 2017, introduced a ban on constructing new nuclear power plants. However, concerns about energy security following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine have revived calls to lift this ban. These efforts culminated in a popular initiative known as “Stop the Blackout,” which collected enough signatures in early 2024 to force political consideration. While the Federal Council declined to put the initiative directly to a referendum, it proposed an indirect counter-proposal, including legislative changes that would repeal the newbuild ban. Political divisions regarding nuclear power will set near-term focus on renewables Consultations on revising the Nuclear Energy Act took place between late 2024 and spring 2025, revealing strong political divisions. Centre-right parties largely supported reopening the option of nuclear construction, while Greens and other left-leaning parties opposed it. Even if the ban were lifted, expert assessments suggest that a new nuclear plant in Switzerland would be unlikely to come online before around 2050, due to long planning, licensing, and construction timelines. Throughout the debate, the government has continued to emphasise that accelerated deployment of renewable energy remains the top priority. Hydro power and renewables form the bulk of Swiss power generation In practice, Switzerland’s electricity system is already dominated by renewables. In 2025, hydropower alone supplied nearly 60% of electricity generation, benefiting from favourable hydrological conditions. Nuclear power contributed just over 28%, while solar power continued its rapid rise, increasing its share to more than 7%, with further growth

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 49 projected by the Swiss Federal Office of Energy in the coming years. Wind power remains marginal, and fossil fuels play only a minor role. Against this backdrop, Switzerland’s nuclear future is defined less by expansion than by managing the risks and costs of an aging fleet during a period of rapid renewable growth. Bulgaria Bulgaria’s nuclear sector is deeply rooted in its Soviet-era energy infrastructure. The country launched its nuclear programme in the 1970s with VVER-440 reactors and later added two larger VVER-1000 units at the Kozloduy nuclear power plant, which remain the backbone of Bulgarian nuclear generation today. Earlier ambitions to expand capacity further, notably through the Belene project, were repeatedly derailed after the collapse of the Soviet Union and subsequent political and economic instability. In 2025, the two operating reactors at Kozloduy generated about 16TWh of electricity, slightly less than the year before. Despite this modest decline in output, nuclear power’s share of Bulgaria’s electricity mix increased to nearly 42%, mainly because coal generation fell sharply. As a result, nuclear energy has strengthened its position as Bulgaria’s single most important source of electricity, underscoring its role in ensuring system stability during a period of structural change in the power sector. Historically, Bulgaria’s energy system was highly dependent on Russia, not only for nuclear fuel but also for fossil fuels, especially natural gas. Before Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, more than 90% of Bulgaria’s gas imports came from Russia. Since then, this dependence has been dramatically reduced. Russian gas supplies for domestic consumption have stopped entirely, and Bulgaria now meets its gas demand primarily through liquefied natural gas imports via Greece and Türkiye, supported by long-term access agreements with the Turkish company BOTAŞ. At the same time, Bulgaria continues to act as a transit country for Russian gas to other parts of Europe through the Balkan Stream pipeline, which reached record transit volumes in 2024. This role, however, has brought limited financial benefit to Bulgaria, as transit fees are low and much of the economic value accrues to Gazprom and intermediaries. Fuel diversification to reduce dependency Fuel diversification has also become a priority in the nuclear sector. Although Kozloduy still relies on Russian-designed equipment, Bulgaria has moved decisively to reduce its dependence on Russian nuclear fuel. A long-term contract with Westinghouse was signed to supply fuel for Unit 5, with the first deliveries arriving in 2024 and further reloads in 2025. The transition to alternative fuel is scheduled to be completed by 2027. In parallel, Kozloduy has contracted Framatome to supply fuel for Unit 6 and initiated licensing work to allow Western fuel designs to be used safely in both reactors. The two VVER-1000 units at Kozloduy are also undergoing lifetime extension programmes. Originally licensed for 30 years, they have already been refurbished to allow operation for up to 60 years. Regulatory changes adopted in 2024 replaced fixed-term operating licenses with indefinite ones, provided that periodic safety reviews are successfully completed. This effectively allows Units 5 and 6 to remain in operation if they meet evolving safety standards, reinforcing nuclear power’s long-term role in Bulgaria’s energy system. New plant planned but timing uncertain At the same time, Bulgaria is pushing forward with plans to build new nuclear capacity at Kozloduy. A project to construct two AP1000 reactors with Westinghouse technology has advanced through feasibility studies, engineering contracts, and vendor selection, with Hyundai Engineering & Construction chosen as the main construction partner. Financing discussions are ongoing, including potential support from the US Export- Import Bank, while cost estimates and final investment decisions remain unresolved. The target date for commissioning the first new unit is the mid-2030s, though timelines remain ambitious. Meanwhile, the long-troubled Belene project continues to cast a shadow over Bulgarian nuclear policy. Although officially cancelled in 2023, debates over selling its equipment to Ukraine, repurposing the site, or even reviving the project in a new form have kept it politically alive. Against this complex backdrop, Bulgaria’s long-term energy strategy positions nuclear power as a cornerstone of energy security, alongside a gradual coal phaseout and a significant expansion of solar and wind power, as the country seeks to balance decarbonisation, independence, and economic stability. The Czech Republic The Czech Republic relies heavily on nuclear power as a cornerstone of its electricity system. It currently operates six Russian-designed reactors at two sites: four VVER-440 units at Dukovany and two larger VVER-1000 units at Temelín. The Dukovany reactors entered service between 1985 and 1987, while both Temelín units were connected to the grid in 2000. Operating licences for all reactors are unlimited in duration but depend on successful periodic safety reviews conducted every 10 years. In 2025, nuclear power generation reached about 32.1TWh, slightly higher than the previous year, and accounted for just over 40% of total electricity production, underscoring nuclear energy’s central role in the Czech power mix. Growth for nuclear capacity planned This importance is reinforced in the country’s updated National Energy and Climate Plan, published in late 2024. The plan foresees nuclear power expanding its share of electricity generation to around 44% by 2030 and potentially peaking at close to 68% by 2040, before declining again to about 46% by mid-century. These projections reflect a strategy built on three pillars: long-term operation of existing reactors, the construction of new large nuclear units, and the eventual deployment of small and intermediate modular reactors. Existing plants are scheduled to operate for at least 60 years, subject to rigorous safety reviews, while new capacity is

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 50 intended to compensate for aging infrastructure and support decarbonisation goals. Fuel diversification to reduce dependency on a single supplier Reducing dependence on Russia has become a key theme in Czech nuclear policy, particularly in the fuel cycle. Historically, Dukovany and Temelín relied on Russian fuel and related services, but this began to change following geopolitical tensions and the war in Ukraine. Fuel delivery contracts with Russia expired in 2024, and alternative supplies were introduced in 2025. Westinghouse delivered the first non-Russian fuel assemblies to both nuclear plants, marking a significant milestone, with mixed-origin fuel initially loaded into the reactor cores. ČEZ, the state-controlled utility operating the plants, has also expanded on-site fuel storage capacity to improve resilience against supply disruptions. Additional diversification steps are underway. ČEZ has agreements with Framatome to develop alternative VVER-1000 fuel designs and signed a long-term contract with Urenco for uranium enrichment services extending into the mid-2030s. In 2025, ČEZ also secured supplies of natural uranium from Kazakhstan’s Kazatomprom, covering a substantial portion of Temelín’s future needs for Westinghouse fuel. Together, these measures aim to establish a more secure and diversified nuclear fuel supply chain, though some options, such as Framatome’s fuel fabrication in Germany under a Russian licence, remain subject to regulatory uncertainty. Plans for new large reactors have been evolving for more than two decades, particularly at the Dukovany site. After a lengthy tender process, bids were submitted by Westinghouse, EDF, and Korea Hydro and Nuclear Power. In mid-2024, ČEZ selected KHNP’s APR-1000 design as the preferred option, and the Czech government endorsed the choice. The project expanded from an initial plan for one unit to two units, with the possibility of additional reactors later, to reduce costs through scale. The announced price for the Dukovany expansion was roughly CZK 400bn for two units, with completion targeted for 2036 and 2037. The decision, however, triggered a series of legal and political challenges. EDF and Westinghouse contested the tender process, raising concerns about intellectual property rights, alleged state subsidies, and fair competition. These disputes led to temporary injunctions and scrutiny by Czech courts and European institutions. Although initial complaints were dismissed and a US–Korean settlement resolved longstanding intellectual property conflicts, the European Commission requested a pause to examine potential foreign subsidies. After further legal proceedings, the Czech Supreme Administrative Court cleared the way in June 2025, allowing the EPC contract with KHNP to be signed. The government subsequently increased its stake in the Dukovany project company to 80%, signalling strong state backing and an ambition to secure a high level of domestic industrial participation. ČEZ to work with Rolls Royce on new SMR capacity Beyond large reactors, the Czech Republic is also positioning itself in the emerging field of small modular reactors. ČEZ plans to deploy up to 3 GW of SMR capacity by 2050, with the first unit envisaged at the Temelín site sometime in the mid-2030s. Progress accelerated in late 2024 when ČEZ entered a strategic partnership with Rolls-Royce SMR, acquiring a significant equity stake and advancing environmental assessments for potential sites. While timelines remain optimistic and dependent on regulatory and technological readiness, SMRs are presented as a flexible complement to large nuclear units. In the broader power system, nuclear energy remains a stabilising force. In 2025, the Czech Republic generated about 71.4TWh of electricity, with fossil fuels still the largest contributor but in marked decline compared to five years earlier. Nuclear output remained stable, while solar generation grew significantly, albeit from a low base, and hydro and wind played smaller roles. Overall electricity production has fallen since 2019, balanced by reduced exports and consumption. Within this evolving landscape, nuclear power is set to remain a central pillar of Czech energy policy for decades to come. Hungary Hungary’s nuclear sector is centred on a single site, the Paks nuclear power plant, where four Soviet-designed VVER-440 reactors remain in operation. In 2025, these units produced about 16TWh of electricity, supplying roughly 47% of the country’s power generation. While output was essentially unchanged from the previous year, nuclear power’s share declined slightly as other sources, particularly solar, expanded rapidly. Nuclear’s contribution had peaked a decade earlier, in 2014, when it exceeded 50% of electricity generation. Fuel diversification more aligned with EU efforts Historically, Hungary has been fully dependent on Russia for nuclear fuel at Paks. This began to change in late 2023, when Parliament amended national nuclear policy to allow alternative fuel sourcing. A major step followed in October 2024, when the government signed a long-term fuel supply contract with Framatome, scheduled to take effect in 2027. This move aligns Hungary with broader European efforts to reduce reliance on Russian nuclear services, even as Russian fuel continues to be used in the short term. The existing reactors, known collectively as Paks I, are currently licensed to operate for 50 years following earlier lifetime extensions. In December 2023, the government notified the European Commission of its intention to extend operations by another 20 years, potentially allowing the reactors to run into the 2050s. Planned investments of around EUR 1.5bn would be required to modernise electrical and control systems. Unlike earlier plans that envisaged shutting down Paks I once new reactors came online, the government now expects Paks I and the planned Paks II units to operate in parallel, raising environmental and technical concerns.

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 51 One particularly sensitive issue is thermal pollution of the Danube River, which provides cooling water for the plant. A regulatory limit of 30°C is intended to protect aquatic ecosystems, but Paks I has already faced output reductions during hot periods and has reportedly exceeded this threshold in the past. In 2024, the government introduced emergency rules allowing continued full-power operation above the limit if energy security is deemed at risk, and in 2025 an advisory body recommended raising the limit by 2°C. How this framework would function once Paks II is operating, effectively doubling nuclear capacity at the site, remains unclear, especially as alternatives such as cooling towers are considered prohibitively expensive. Rosatom to supply two reactors. No public tender submitted by Rosatom Plans for Paks II have been under discussion for more than 15 years. After Parliament approved expansion in principle in 2009, the project took a decisive turn in 2014 when Hungary awarded a contract without a public tender to Russia’s Rosatom to build two VVER-1200 reactors, financed largely by a Russian state loan. The project has since been mired in legal, financial, and political controversy, including challenges over state aid and procurement. While the European Commission approved the project, Austria challenged the decision in court, and in early 2025 the Advocate General of the EU Court of Justice recommended overturning a prior ruling that had upheld the aid approval. A final judgment is still pending. Continuation of Paks II despite sanctions Despite EU sanctions on Russia, Hungary has secured exemptions to keep Paks II moving forward. Key milestones were reached in 2024 and 2025, including delivery of major components, approval of the preliminary safety analysis report, and partial authorisation to resume construction after a structural incident at the site. Uncertainty persists over the supply of control systems, particularly the role of Siemens Energy, which has faced export licensing hurdles in Germany but is now seeking to deliver equipment through its Hungarian operations. Alongside nuclear, Hungary has experienced a dramatic surge in solar power. Installed solar capacity grew from negligible levels a decade ago to 8.4GW by 2025, enabling solar to supply about a quarter of Hungary’s electricity, one of the highest shares globally. This rapid growth has reshaped the power mix and reduced nuclear’s relative dominance. Hungary’s energy strategy envisions continued nuclear operation combined with expanding renewables, though grid constraints and unresolved issues around Paks II mean the long-term balance between these sources remains uncertain. Romania Romania’s nuclear power sector is centred on a single site at Cernavodă, where two Canadian-designed CANDU heavy-water reactors with a combined capacity of about 1.3GW are in operation. In 2024, these units produced electricity of roughly 10.4TWh net, corresponding to c. 20% of national generation, slightly below the previous year and just under the historical peak reached in 2020. Nuclear power thus remains a major pillar of Romania’s energy system, though it now operates alongside a rapidly changing mix dominated by renewables. Nuclear is second-largest source electricity generation Hydropower remains Romania’s leading electricity source, supplying more than a quarter of total generation. Nuclear followed as the second-largest contributor, ahead of natural gas and coal. Among non-hydro renewables, wind power played the most significant role, while solar photovoltaic generation, though still smaller, grew rapidly. The solar share nearly doubled between 2023 and 2024 as more than 1.7GW of new capacity was added, and further large additions are expected in the coming years. Romania is also rolling out battery storage at scale, targeting up to 5GW by the end of 2026 to improve system flexibility. Romania’s updated National Energy and Climate Plan sets out an ambitious trajectory towards a low-carbon electricity system. By 2030, renewables are expected to account for three-quarters of electricity generation, supported by major expansions of solar, wind and hydro capacity, and a complete phase-out of coal by 2032. Within this framework, nuclear energy is also set to expand. The plan assumes continued operation and modest uprating of the existing Cernavodă reactors, the deployment of SMRs, and the construction of two additional large reactors at the same site, which together would raise total nuclear capacity to more than 3GW by the mid-2030s. SMR strategy proceeding Romania’s SMR programme has attracted international attention as a potential first-of-a-kind deployment in Europe. The project, based on NuScale’s 77MW modules, targets a former coal plant site at Doicești and envisions six modules delivering a total of 462MW. Preparatory studies and licensing work have been ongoing for several years, supported by US government institutions. In 2024 and 2025, design and engineering activities advanced further, and financing discussions continued. However, a final investment decision has not yet been taken, and timelines have gradually slipped. While official statements still point to first operation around the end of the decade, many uncertainties remain, including the fact that NuScale has not yet built a reactor anywhere and that design licensing in Romania is still incomplete. At Cernavodă itself, maintaining the existing nuclear fleet is a major priority. Like other aging CANDU reactors worldwide, Unit 1 requires extensive refurbishment to allow operation beyond its original 30-year design life. After years of preparation, Romania signed a major engineering, procurement, and construction contract in late 2024 with an international consortium to carry out the refurbishment. The project, estimated to cost several billion euros in total, is scheduled to involve a multi-year outage from 2027 onward, after which the unit would return to service for up to another 30 years.

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 52 Meanwhile, long-running plans to add new large reactors at Cernavodă have regained momentum. Preliminary contracts for managing construction of Units 3 and 4 were signed in late 2024, supported by substantial international financing commitments and state guarantees. Although political leaders have reiterated ambitions to bring both units online in the early 2030s, the long construction schedules and unresolved financial and contractual steps suggest that achieving these dates will be challenging. Overall, Romania is pursuing an expansive nuclear agenda alongside fast-growing renewables, but the pace of implementation remains uncertain across all major projects. Nuclear power makes up 61% of Slovakian power generation Slovakia Slovakia remains one of the most nuclear-dependent countries in the world, with nuclear power providing well over half of its electricity for more than two decades. This reliance deepened further after the commissioning of the third reactor at the Mochovce nuclear power plant, which helped lift the nuclear share of electricity generation to more than 61% in both 2023 and 2024. Nuclear output remained broadly stable in 2024, reinforcing Slovakia’s position as the country with the second-highest nuclear share globally, behind only France. Electricity generation from nuclear power is concentrated in two sites operated by Slovenské elektrárne. The utility is now jointly owned by the Czech-based EPH group, holding a two-thirds stake, and the Slovak state, which owns the remaining third. At Jaslovské Bohunice, two VVER-440 reactors that entered service in the mid-1980s continue to operate after extensive modernisation and capacity uprates completed over the past two decades. At Mochovce, three reactors of similar design are connected to the grid, providing a combined national nuclear capacity of around 2.3GW, while a fourth unit is in the final stages of testing ahead of commissioning. The long and costly Mochovce expansion has shaped Slovakia’s nuclear trajectory. Construction of Mochovce-3 and -4 began in the 1980s, was suspended in the early 1990s, and resumed only after Italy’s Enel acquired a controlling stake in Slovenské elektrárne in 2006. Mochovce-3 eventually entered commercial operation in 2023, more than a decade later than previously planned, after years of delays and cost overruns that drove total project costs to more than double initial estimates. Mochovce-4 progressed through final testing phases in 2024-25 with a delayed start until 2026, with the utility emphasising that completing this unit remains its most urgent short-term priority. A revival of new nuclear plans Looking ahead, Slovakia plans to operate its existing reactors well into mid-century. Bohunice Units 3 and 4 are expected to operate until the mid-2040s, while Mochovce Units 1 and 2 could remain online until close to 2060. At the same time, the government has revived plans for new nuclear capacity at Bohunice. A state-owned project envisions a large, roughly 1200MW reactor, with construction potentially starting in the early 2030s and commissioning towards the end of the decade or later. Cost estimates have risen sharply, and the procurement process remains politically contentious, with opposition parties criticising the lack of transparency and warning against sidelining competitive tendering. Alongside large reactors, Slovakia is also exploring small and advanced modular reactors. Several feasibility studies are under way, backed by US government grants, to assess potential sites and technologies that could repurpose former coal plants. Partnerships with Western vendors such as Westinghouse, EDF, and new entrants developing advanced reactor designs highlight Slovakia’s ambition to position itself as a future hub for modular nuclear technologies, although timelines remain vague and no final investment decisions have been taken. Despite these ambitions, Slovakia’s energy policy is marked by contradictions. The country remains heavily dependent on Russian energy, including nuclear fuel, gas and oil, even as it pursues diversification agreements with Western suppliers starting later this decade. Political choices under the current government have reinforced these dependencies, complicating efforts to meet European Union energy and climate objectives. Meanwhile, renewables play only a minor role in Slovakia’s power mix, with hydropower accounting for a modest share and solar and wind lagging well behind EU averages. This imbalance underscores how decisively Slovakia continues to place nuclear energy at the centre of its electricity system, even as broader decarbonisation challenges remain unresolved. Nuclear power output corresponds to 34% of Slovenian power generation Slovenia Slovenia’s electricity system is built around a relatively balanced mix of nuclear power, hydropower and fossil fuels, though nuclear energy remains a central pillar. In 2024, nuclear power accounted for about 34% of electricity generation, hydropower for roughly 31%, and coal for just over 20%, while solar photovoltaics continued to grow, reaching nearly 9%. Although fossil fuels still represented almost a quarter of the power mix, their role is expected to diminish significantly as Slovenia moves toward its long-term climate goals. The single operational nuclear plant has seen lifetime extension The country operates a single nuclear power plant at Krško, jointly owned with Croatia. The 688MW pressurised water reactor, built by Westinghouse, has been in operation since 1983 and supplies electricity equally to both countries. In 2024, Krško generated around 5.8TWh of electricity, slightly increasing its absolute output compared to the previous year but accounting for a marginally smaller share of total generation due to growth in other sources. After refurbishment and safety upgrades, the plant’s operating license was extended to 60 years, allowing operation until 2043. International safety reviews by the IAEA have supported

The European nuclear landscape 27 March 2026 53 long-term operation, while also recommending further improvements in aging management and documentation of critical systems. Alongside extending the life of Krško, Slovenia is pursuing plans for a second nuclear unit at the same site, known as JEK2. Discussions about a new reactor date back almost two decades, with project concepts evolving from multiple large units to a single reactor of flexible size. In recent years, nuclear newbuild has been framed as a key tool for decarbonising the power sector and replacing coal, which Slovenia plans to phase out completely by 2033. Updated energy and climate strategies call for a major increase in renewable energy, combined with a decision on new nuclear capacity by the late 2020s. Political debate around JEK2 has been intense. While Parliament adopted a resolution in 2024 emphasising nuclear energy’s long-term role, a planned consultative referendum was later cancelled due to legal concerns and calls for clearer public information. The government has committed to holding a referendum before any final investment decision, likely before 2028. Meanwhile, feasibility studies are underway with EDF and Westinghouse as the remaining potential technology suppliers, after a Korean vendor withdrew. Looking ahead, Slovenia aims to reach climate neutrality by 2050, with renewables supplying most of the electricity by 2030. Nuclear power is expected to complement this transition by providing stable, low-carbon generation, even as costs, public acceptance, and long timelines continue to shape the country’s energy debate. United Kingdom Since the election of a new government in the summer of 2024, the United Kingdom has reshaped its energy and climate policy framework with the stated aim of improving energy security while accelerating decarbonisation. Central to this approach has been a renewed political commitment to nuclear power. The government has made additional public funds available for the construction of two large reactors at Sizewell C, for the development of SMRs, and for nuclear fusion research. This renewed backing comes despite the continuation of long-standing problems in the sector, notably chronic cost overruns, prolonged construction delays, and mounting expenses associated with maintaining an aging reactor fleet. UK reactor units’ startups and closures Source: WNISR Aging British nuclear fleet By mid-2025, the UK operated only nine nuclear reactors, with a combined capacity of roughly 5.8GW. All but one of these units are Advanced Gas-Cooled Reactors (AGRs), a design unique to Britain, built in the 1980s. The sole pressurised water reactor, Sizewell B, entered service in 1995. The average age of the operating fleet exceeded 38 years, highlighting the maturity of existing assets. Since 2018, closures have steadily reduced capacity, including the shutdown of Dungeness B, Hunterston B, and Hinkley Point B. Altogether, 36 reactors have been closed, leaving nearly 8GW in various stages of decommissioning. Age of UK nuclear fleet Source: IAEA-PRIS (2025) Nuclear generation in 2024 amounted to around 40TWh, providing just over 14% of electricity supply, half the contribution of wind and far below nuclear’s historical peak share of 28% in the late 1990s. Eight AGR units continue to operate at Heysham, Hartlepool and Torness, while EDF, which owns the fleet, has pushed for limited lifetime extensions. In late 2024, EDF announced plans to operate several AGRs into the late 2020s and early 2030s, though it acknowledged that uncertainty over graphite core degradation makes future output unpredictable. Hartlepool was placed under enhanced regulatory scrutiny in 2025, reflecting concerns about safety management and aging infrastructure.
